S01.title: Unpot and Rinse Roots
S01.body: Slide the plant out of its pot, gently shake off loose soil, and rinse the root ball under lukewarm running water until you can clearly see individual roots.

S02.title: Sterilize Cutting Tools
S02.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

S03.title: Trim Damaged or Dead Roots
S03.body: Cut away black, mushy, or shriveled roots with sterilized pruners. Healthy roots are white or tan and pliable. Cut back to clean, firm tissue.

S04.title: Prune Infected Leaves and Stems
S04.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

S05.title: Pinch Off Affected Flowers and Buds
S05.body: Use clean fingers or sterilized snips to remove rotting, wilted, or infected blooms at their base, before infection spreads to surrounding healthy buds.

S06.title: Remove and Destroy Infected Fruits
S06.body: Pick all spotted, rotted, or moldy fruit from the plant and from the ground beneath. Bury (12 inches deep), burn, or seal in a trash bag — do not compost.

S07.title: Hand-Pick Pests Into Soapy Water
S07.body: Wear gloves. Pick caterpillars, beetles, slugs, or egg clusters off by hand and drop them into a bucket of soapy water. Drown for at least 24 hours, then dispose.

S08.title: Wipe Pests Off With Damp Cloth
S08.body: For mealybugs, scale crawlers, or aphids, gently wipe leaves and stems with a soft cloth dipped in soapy water. Re-rinse the cloth often. Avoid bruising tender tissue.

S09.title: Repot With Fresh Soil and Drainage
S09.body: Move the plant to a clean pot with drainage holes, using fresh sterile potting mix. Bury roots up to the same depth as before. Water lightly to settle the soil.

S10.title: Up-Pot to a Larger Container
S10.body: Move a root-bound plant into a pot 1–2 sizes larger. Tease apart circling roots before settling the plant in fresh mix. Water deeply once.

S11.title: Top-Dress With Sand or Gravel
S11.body: Scrape away the top 1/8 inch of moldy or salt-crusted soil and replace it with a 1/4 inch layer of clean coarse sand or fine gravel. Water sparingly afterward.

S12.title: Solarize the Soil
S12.body: Lay a clear plastic sheet over moist garden soil for 4–6 weeks during the hottest part of the year. Heat builds underneath and kills nematodes, fungi, and weed seeds.

S13.title: Apply Organic Mulch Around Base
S13.body: Spread a 2–3 inch layer of bark, straw, or leaf mulch around the plant, keeping a small ring clear from the stem. Mulch blocks splash-up of soil pathogens and stabilizes moisture.

S14.title: Flush Soil to Leach Salts
S14.body: Take the plant to a sink or outdoors. Pour clean water slowly through the pot — about 4 times the pot's volume — until water runs freely from the drainage holes. Repeat once a month if salts return.

S15.title: Water at Base, Never Overhead
S15.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

S16.title: Reduce Watering Frequency
S16.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

S17.title: Water Deeply But Less Often
S17.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

S18.title: Move to a Brighter Location
S18.body: Relocate the plant gradually to a south- or west-facing window or a sunnier spot in the garden. Move in stages over a week to avoid sun shock.

S19.title: Move to a Shaded Location
S19.body: Relocate the plant to a north-facing window, under a tree, or behind shade cloth. Sunburn and leaf scorch usually heal once the plant cools off.

S20.title: Add a Grow Light
S20.body: Hang or stand a full-spectrum LED grow light 30–60 cm above the plant, on a 12–14 hour timer. Especially useful in winter or in dim rooms.

S21.title: Increase Airflow and Spacing
S21.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

S22.title: Increase Humidity Around Plant
S22.body: Mist the foliage daily, group plants together, set the pot on a tray of pebbles in shallow water, or run a humidifier. Aim for 40–60% relative humidity.

S23.title: Protect From Frost
S23.body: Cover vulnerable plants with a frost cloth, burlap, sheet, or row cover before nightfall. Bring potted plants indoors. Remove the cover the next morning.

S24.title: Stake or Trellis the Plant
S24.body: Drive a wooden or bamboo stake into the soil near the plant and tie loose figure-eight loops with soft twine. Keeps stems off the wet ground and improves airflow.

S25.title: Apply Copper Fungicide
S25.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

S26.title: Apply Broad-Spectrum Fungicide
S26.body: When copper is not enough, use a synthetic systemic fungicide (e.g. propiconazole, myclobutanil) per the product label. Spray to runoff. Always rotate fungicides to avoid resistance.

S27.title: Apply Neem Oil
S27.body: Mix cold-pressed neem oil with water and a few drops of dish soap as an emulsifier (typical 2 tsp neem + 1 tsp soap per quart). Spray to runoff in the cool of evening.

S28.title: Apply Insecticidal Soap
S28.body: Dilute 1 tablespoon of mild liquid dish soap (no degreasers) per quart of water in a spray bottle. Coat the undersides and tops of leaves until dripping. Re-spray every 2–3 days for 1–2 weeks.

S29.title: Apply Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt)
S29.body: Spray Bt on leaves and stems where caterpillars feed. Caterpillars must ingest it. Bt is harmless to bees, pets, and humans. Reapply after rain.

S30.title: Apply Pyrethrin Spray
S30.body: Use a pyrethrin or pyrethrum (chrysanthemum-derived) insecticide per label. Spray in the evening to protect bees. Effective on thrips, leafhoppers, and beetles.

S31.title: Apply Systemic Insecticide
S31.body: For stubborn or hidden pests, drench the soil with a systemic (imidacloprid, dinotefuran) per label. The plant absorbs it through roots, making sap toxic to sucking insects. Avoid on flowering plants visited by bees.

S32.title: Spray Pests Off With Strong Hose
S32.body: Use a hose nozzle on a stream setting to blast aphids, mites, mealybugs, or lace bugs off leaves and stems. Aim under leaves too. Repeat every few days.

S33.title: Release Beneficial Insects
S33.body: Buy ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps, or predatory mites from a garden supplier. Release in the evening near the affected plant. Provide nectar plants nearby to keep them around.

S34.title: Set Pheromone Traps
S34.body: Hang species-specific pheromone traps in or near affected plants. Traps catch male moths, suppressing the next generation. Replace lures every 4–6 weeks during the season.

S35.title: Use Insect Netting or Row Covers
S35.body: Drape fine mesh netting or floating row covers over plants and secure the edges with stones or pins. Keeps moths, beetles, and caterpillars from laying eggs.

S36.title: Apply Balanced Fertilizer
S36.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

S37.title: Apply Iron-Rich Foliar Feed
S37.body: Mix a chelated iron product per label and spray directly on yellowed leaves. Faster than soil drench. Repeat every 2–3 weeks until green returns.

S38.title: Test and Amend Soil pH
S38.body: Use a soil test kit or send a sample to your local extension office. Add elemental sulfur to lower pH or garden lime to raise it, per the test recommendation.

S39.title: Inspect and Quarantine New Plants
S39.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

S40.title: Choose Resistant Varieties
S40.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

S41.title: Clear Debris Around Plants
S41.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

S42.title: Rotate Crops Between Seasons
S42.body: Do not plant the same species — or its botanical relatives — in the same spot two years in a row. A 3-year rotation breaks the lifecycle of soil-borne diseases and pests.

S43.title: Discard the Severely Infected Plant
S43.body: When more than half the plant is affected or root rot is widespread, dig the plant up, seal it in a heavy trash bag, and dispose. Sterilize the area before replanting.

S44.title: Sterilize Pots Before Reuse
S44.body: Soak empty pots for 10 minutes in a 1:9 bleach-to-water solution, then rinse thoroughly with clean water and air-dry before refilling with fresh soil.

K01.title: Hydrogen Peroxide Soil Drench
K01.recipe: 1 part 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide to 3 parts water. (Or 1 part 35% food-grade peroxide to 30 parts water.)
K01.usage: Pour around the base of the plant until the topsoil is moistened. Repeat once a week until the soil dries normally and bubbling stops.

K02.title: Cinnamon Powder
K02.recipe: Plain ground cinnamon from the spice rack. No mixing.
K02.usage: Dust a thin layer over moldy soil, on freshly cut stems, or around the base of seedlings. Cinnamon is naturally antifungal and rooting-friendly.

K03.title: Apple Cider Vinegar Trap
K03.recipe: Pour 1/4 cup apple cider vinegar into a small yellow cup or saucer. Add 1 drop of dish soap to break surface tension. Cover with plastic wrap and poke small holes.
K03.usage: Place near the affected plant. Adult fungus gnats and fruit flies are attracted, fall in, and drown. Refresh every 3–4 days.

K04.title: Chamomile Tea Drench
K04.recipe: Steep 2 chamomile tea bags in 1 quart of just-boiled water. Cool to room temperature.
K04.usage: Pour around the base of seedlings and over the soil surface. Mild antifungal action protects germinating seeds and young roots.

K05.title: Baking Soda + Dish Soap Spray
K05.recipe: 1 teaspoon baking soda + 1 teaspoon mild dish soap + 1 quart water. Shake to dissolve.
K05.usage: Spray on leaves at the first sign of mildew, every 7 days. Coat upper and lower leaf surfaces. Spot-test first; some plants get leaf burn.

K06.title: Milk Spray
K06.recipe: 1 part milk (any fat, but whole works best) to 9 parts water in a spray bottle.
K06.usage: Spray on leaves once a week in bright weather. Sunlight activates milk proteins into a mild antifungal coat.

K07.title: Garlic Spray
K07.recipe: Crush 4 garlic cloves into 1 quart of water. Steep overnight. Strain. Add 1 tsp dish soap.
K07.usage: Spray on leaves and stems, focusing on undersides. Repeat every 5–7 days. Strong-smelling for a day, then dissipates.

K08.title: Chili Pepper Spray
K08.recipe: 2 tablespoons cayenne or crushed dried chili in 1 quart of water. Boil 10 minutes. Cool. Strain. Add 1 tsp dish soap.
K08.usage: Spray on leaves where caterpillars or beetles are feeding. Wear gloves and eye protection while spraying.

K09.title: Coffee Grounds Barrier
K09.recipe: Used coffee grounds dried out on a tray.
K09.usage: Sprinkle a 1/2 inch ring around the base of slug-prone plants. Rough texture and caffeine repel slugs and snails. Replace after rain.

K10.title: Crushed Eggshell
K10.recipe: Rinsed eggshells, dried, crushed coarsely with a rolling pin.
K10.usage: (a) Mix into soil at planting for slow calcium release — helps prevent blossom-end rot. (b) Sprinkle a sharp-edged ring around plants — slugs avoid the edges.

K11.title: Beer Trap
K11.recipe: Bury a shallow dish or cup with the rim level with the soil. Fill 2/3 with cheap beer. (Or 1 cup water + 1 tsp dry yeast + 1 tsp sugar.)
K11.usage: Place near affected plants in the evening. Slugs and snails crawl in and drown. Refresh every 2–3 days.

K12.title: Banana Peel Potassium Tea
K12.recipe: Steep 2–3 chopped banana peels in 1 quart of water for 48 hours. Strain.
K12.usage: Pour around the base of stressed or yellowing plants every 2–3 weeks. Light potassium boost; not a complete fertilizer.

K13.title: Rubbing Alcohol Cotton Swab
K13.recipe: 70% isopropyl rubbing alcohol straight from the bottle.
K13.usage: Dip a cotton swab and dab directly on individual mealybugs or scale insects. Kills on contact. Avoid soaking the leaf.

K14.title: Plain Soapy Water Spray
K14.recipe: 1 teaspoon mild liquid dish soap (no degreasers, no additives) per quart of water in a spray bottle.
K14.usage: Spray to drench all surfaces, especially leaf undersides, in the evening. Rinse with plain water the next morning. Re-spray every 3 days for 2 weeks.

K15.title: Aspirin Water Foliar Spray
K15.recipe: 1 uncoated aspirin tablet (325 mg) dissolved in 1 gallon of water with a few drops of dish soap.
K15.usage: Spray on leaves every 2–3 weeks. Salicylic acid in aspirin gently stimulates the plant's own disease-defense response.

L01.name: Brown spot
L01.shortDescription: Brown blotches or tiny brown dots on leaves
L01.symptomAnalysis:
Brown spot is a catch-all label for fungal and bacterial infections that leave brown lesions on leaves. Causes vary, but conditions are usually similar: wet leaves, poor airflow, and contaminated debris carrying spores. If left alone, brown spot weakens the plant and opens it to more damaging diseases.

Whether the spot is one of three patterns (large blotches, blotches with yellow spots, or tiny dense dots) doesn't change the treatment.

L02.shortDescription: Black blotches or densely-packed black dots on leaves
L02.symptomAnalysis: Most often a fungal infection — *Diplocarpon rosae* on roses is the textbook example, but other fungi cause similar damage on different hosts. The fungus thrives in wet conditions and spreads when water splashes spores onto leaves. Mild cases are mostly cosmetic; severe ones defoliate the plant and weaken it for the next season.

L03.name: Leaf blight & scorch
L03.shortDescription: Tips and edges dry brown, yellow halo
L03.symptomAnalysis:
Three causes:

1. **Heat and sun stress** — leaves lose water faster than roots can supply. Sun-facing leaves brown first.
2. **Poor root function** — compacted soil, root damage, or excess fertilizer prevents roots from absorbing enough water. The plant looks scorched even on a normal day.
3. **Bacterial leaf scorch** (*Xylella fastidiosa*) — chronic disease that recurs each year. Affects older leaves first; newer leaves stay clean. Permanent.

Once leaves brown, they don't recover. Goal is to save the rest of the plant.

steps.title: Use shade cloth or move container plants
steps.body: Relocate the plant to a north-facing window, under a tree, or behind shade cloth. Sunburn and leaf scorch usually heal once the plant cools off.

steps.body: Spread a 2–3 inch layer of bark, straw, or leaf mulch around the plant, keeping a small ring clear from the stem. Mulch blocks splash-up of soil pathogens and stabilizes moisture.

L04.name: Anthracnose
L04.shortDescription: Gray blotches with dark brown or black edges
L04.symptomAnalysis:
Anthracnose is a group of related fungal diseases. All thrive in cool, wet weather, especially spring and early summer. Spores overwinter in plant debris and infect new growth as conditions warm. Common on shade trees, fruit trees, beans, tomatoes, and many ornamentals.

Severe outbreaks defoliate plants. Mild ones are cosmetic.

L05.name: Angular leaf spot
L05.shortDescription: Yellow spots squared off by leaf veins
L05.symptomAnalysis:
A bacterial disease (*Pseudomonas* spp.) seed-borne but spread further by warm, moist weather and overhead watering. Veins block the bacteria from spreading freely, so lesions stay constrained to a single panel between veins, giving the squared-off look. Severe cases drop leaves; lesions also appear on stems and fruit.

The disease persists in seeds, plant debris, and contaminated tools.

L06.name: Leaf spot
L06.shortDescription: White or yellow spots scattered across leaves
L06.symptomAnalysis:
A general label for fungal infections producing pale spots on leaves. Many fungi cause similar damage; treatment is roughly the same regardless of the exact species. Spots tend to dry out from the center.

If the spots are tiny and densely packed (under 1 mm), the cause is more likely sucking insects — see [L24 — Sap-sucking damage](sap-sucking-damage.md).

L07.name: Underwatering yellowing
L07.shortDescription: Lower leaves yellow, soil dry and pulling from pot
L07.symptomAnalysis:
When the plant runs out of water, it sacrifices its oldest leaves first to keep newer growth alive. Soil that is dry to your second knuckle and visibly shrinking from the pot edge confirms underwatering. Common in herbs and any plant that has been neglected.

Distinguishes from waterlogging: dry soil + lower leaves yellowing only = under. Wet soil + lower leaves yellowing + soil sour-smelling = over (see [L08](waterlogging-yellow.md)).

steps.title: Pour water slowly until it drains through the bottom
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: If the soil is so dry it repels water: place the pot in a basin of water for 30 minutes to soak from the bottom
Trim off fully dead leaves
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Research the watering needs of each plant
steps.body: Some like dry, some like consistently moist.

steps.title: Set a calendar reminder if you tend to forget

L08.name: Waterlogging
L08.shortDescription: Lower leaves yellow then blacken, soil constantly wet
L08.symptomAnalysis:
Too much water displaces oxygen in the soil, suffocating roots. Rotted roots can no longer take up water, so the leaves above show drought-like symptoms while sitting in soggy soil. The plant trims its lower leaves first to reduce demand.

If left, the rot spreads and the plant dies. May also appear as soft, blackened, rotting leaves rather than just yellow — same cause, more advanced.

For the root-end symptom see [R01 — Root rot](../05_roots/root-rot.md).

steps.title: Stop watering until the top 2 inches of soil are dry
steps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

steps.title: Move to better airflow if indoors
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Trim off the worst yellow leaves
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Unpot and inspect the roots
steps.body: Slide the plant out of its pot, gently shake off loose soil, and rinse the root ball under lukewarm running water until you can clearly see individual roots.

steps.title: Trim away rotted roots
steps.body: Cut away black, mushy, or shriveled roots with sterilized pruners. Healthy roots are white or tan and pliable. Cut back to clean, firm tissue.

steps.title: Repot in fresh dry soil with drainage
steps.body: Move the plant to a clean pot with drainage holes, using fresh sterile potting mix. Bury roots up to the same depth as before. Water lightly to settle the soil.

steps.title: Prune up to 1/3 of healthy leaves to reduce water demand
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Water only when the topsoil is dry
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Use pots with drainage holes
steps.body: Move the plant to a clean pot with drainage holes, using fresh sterile potting mix. Bury roots up to the same depth as before. Water lightly to settle the soil.

steps.title: Use high-quality potting mix; cheap soil compacts and stays soggy
Don't let pots sit in saucers full of water

L09.name: Aged yellow & dry
L09.shortDescription: A few basal leaves yellowing on a healthy plant
L09.symptomAnalysis:
This is normal aging. Plants discard their oldest leaves periodically. Yellowing limited to the lowest leaves on an otherwise healthy plant — with no spots, no pests, no blackening, no widespread effect — means the plant is simply rotating its foliage.

Distinguishes from underwatering: dry-yellowing only affects 1–3 lowest leaves at a time, and replacements grow normally above. Underwatering yellows many leaves at once and the soil is dry to the second knuckle.

steps.title: Pinch off the yellow leaves once they're fully dry
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Don't let dropped leaves sit in the soil
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

L10.name: General chlorosis
L10.shortDescription: Whole plant turning yellow — nutrient deficiency
L10.symptomAnalysis:
Chlorosis means leaves can't make enough chlorophyll. Causes:

- **Nitrogen (N) deficiency** — older inner leaves yellow first; spreads outward if severe.
- **Potassium (K) deficiency** — leaf edges brown and crinkle, with a yellow margin just inside.
- **Zinc deficiency** — yellowing at the base of leaves.
- **Copper / boron** — newer leaves yellow first; foliage becomes brittle.

For specific patterns:

- Yellow between veins, veins stay green → [L11 — Iron deficiency](iron-deficiency.md)
- Yellow veins, leaf body green → [L12 — Veinal chlorosis](veinal-chlorosis.md)

Causes of nutrient unavailability include actual lack in soil, root damage, and wrong soil pH.

steps.title: Apply a balanced fertilizer per label
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

steps.title: Or foliar-feed for fast uptake
steps.body: Mix a chelated iron product per label and spray directly on yellowed leaves. Faster than soil drench. Repeat every 2–3 weeks until green returns.

steps.title: Test soil pH if regular feeding doesn't help
steps.body: Use a soil test kit or send a sample to your local extension office. Add elemental sulfur to lower pH or garden lime to raise it, per the test recommendation.

steps.title: Add compost; supplies micronutrients and improves soil structure

homeRemedies.title: Banana Peel Potassium Tea
homeRemedies.recipe: Steep 2–3 chopped banana peels in 1 quart of water for 48 hours. Strain.
homeRemedies.usage: Pour around the base of stressed or yellowing plants every 2–3 weeks. Light potassium boost; not a complete fertilizer.

steps.title: Regular feeding through the growing season
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

L11.name: Iron deficiency
L11.shortDescription: Yellow between veins, veins stay green (interveinal)
L11.symptomAnalysis:
Classic interveinal chlorosis. Iron is required to make chlorophyll, and leaves can't synthesize new pigment without it. Often the soil has plenty of iron but the plant can't absorb it because the soil pH is too high (alkaline) or compacted.

Common in citrus, blueberries, hydrangeas, gardenias, and many ornamentals on alkaline soils.

steps.title: Foliar spray with chelated iron
steps.body: Mix a chelated iron product per label and spray directly on yellowed leaves. Faster than soil drench. Repeat every 2–3 weeks until green returns.

steps.title: Apply chelated iron to the soil for longer-term correction
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

steps.title: Test pH
steps.body: Use a soil test kit or send a sample to your local extension office. Add elemental sulfur to lower pH or garden lime to raise it, per the test recommendation.

steps.title: Add compost or other organic matter; helps make iron available
Test soil before planting

Don't overlime garden beds; raises pH

L12.name: Veinal chlorosis
L12.shortDescription: Veins turn yellow, rest of leaf stays green
L12.symptomAnalysis:
Veinal chlorosis is less common than interveinal. Causes include:

- **Magnesium or copper deficiency**
- **Soil compaction** preventing root nutrient uptake
- **Mosaic viruses or plant disease** affecting nutrient transport
- **Cold-stress on citrus** during cool seasons (often resolves with warmth)

Less specific than iron deficiency, so diagnosis usually starts with a soil test.

steps.title: Soil test first
steps.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

steps.title: Based on test, apply the right deficient nutrient
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

steps.title: Loosen compacted soil with a fork; mix in compost for aeration

L13.name: Insufficient light
L13.shortDescription: Pale, leggy growth reaching for light (etiolation)
L13.symptomAnalysis:
Etiolation happens when a plant grows in low light. It produces etioplasts instead of chlorophyll, and stems lengthen abnormally as the plant tries to reach light. The result is pale, weak growth that is more vulnerable to disease and pests.

Common in: indoor plants placed too far from a window, seedlings under inadequate grow lights, outdoor plants shaded by overgrown neighbors.

steps.title: Move gradually to a south- or west-facing window
steps.body: Relocate the plant gradually to a south- or west-facing window or a sunnier spot in the garden. Move in stages over a week to avoid sun shock.

steps.title: If natural light is unavailable, add a grow light on a 12–14 hour timer

L14.name: Bacterial leaf rot
L14.symptomAnalysis:
Bacterial leaf rot is caused by various bacteria entering through wounds, stomata (during wet weather), or via splashed soil. Once inside, bacteria multiply quickly and break down healthy tissue. Most common in wet, warm conditions.

Spreads rapidly between plants if not addressed. Differs from fungal infections in the soft, watery, smelly nature of the rot.

steps.title: Cut affected leaves back to clean tissue
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Sterilize between every cut
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Apply copper bactericide to remaining foliage and surrounding soil
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

steps.title: Remove and dispose of the plant
steps.body: When more than half the plant is affected or root rot is widespread, dig the plant up, seal it in a heavy trash bag, and dispose. Sterilize the area before replanting.

steps.title: Mulch to stop soil splash onto leaves
steps.body: Spread a 2–3 inch layer of bark, straw, or leaf mulch around the plant, keeping a small ring clear from the stem. Mulch blocks splash-up of soil pathogens and stabilizes moisture.

Crop rotation breaks bacterial buildup
steps.body: Do not plant the same species — or its botanical relatives — in the same spot two years in a row. A 3-year rotation breaks the lifecycle of soil-borne diseases and pests.

L15.name: Leaf deformity
L15.shortDescription: Misshapen, stunted, bumpy or twisted leaves
L15.symptomAnalysis:
Several causes:

- **Insect damage** — mites, aphids, and other sap-suckers leave plants vulnerable to viruses that distort growth. Galls (raised growths) come from gall-forming insects — see [L16](leaf-gall.md).
- **Herbicide drift** — even small exposure to lawn or driveway herbicides causes characteristic narrow, distorted new leaves and cupping.
- **Cold damage at bud stage** — frost during emerging-leaf stage causes lifelong distortion in those leaves.
- **Water stress** — too much or too little water during the growing phase distorts leaves.
- **Nutrient deficiency** — boron, calcium, and molybdenum shortages cause stunting.

Look at where the deformity is (only new leaves? only some plants?) to narrow it down.

steps.title: Remove deformed leaves so the plant focuses energy on new ones
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Identify the cause:
steps.body: - Pests visible → see [02_pests/](../02_pests/) and treat - All plants in a row affected → herbicide drift; eliminate exposure - Only after a frost → cold damage; wait it out - With yellowing → see [L10 — General chlorosis](chlorosis-fertilizer.md)

steps.title: Apply balanced fertilizer if nutrients are suspect

L16.name: Leaf gall
L16.symptomAnalysis:
Galls form when plant tissue grows abnormally in response to:

- **Sap-sucking insects** — mites, aphids, gall wasps lay eggs or feed inside leaves.
- **Bacterial or fungal infection** — some pathogens trigger gall formation.

The gall itself usually doesn't seriously damage the plant. The underlying cause might. Galls are most common on new growth and after mild winters that didn't kill the gall-makers.

steps.title: Prune off galled leaves and branches
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

L17.name: Low humidity
L17.shortDescription: Leaf edges curling, brown crispy tips
L17.symptomAnalysis:
When the air is too dry, leaves lose water faster than the roots can replace it. The plant curls its leaves to reduce transpiration. Tips and edges, where water arrives last, dry up first.

Common causes: home heating in winter, air conditioning in summer, dry climates. Tropical plants (calathea, fern, fiddle-leaf fig, peace lily, prayer plant) are especially sensitive.

Distinguishes from underwatering: low humidity causes curling and brown tips even when soil is moist. Underwatering also dries soil.

steps.body: Mist the foliage daily, group plants together, set the pot on a tray of pebbles in shallow water, or run a humidifier. Aim for 40–60% relative humidity.

L18.name: Peach leaf curl
L18.shortDescription: New leaves curl, redden, and pucker (fungal)
L18.symptomAnalysis:
A fungal disease (*Taphrina deformans*) that infects emerging leaves in spring. The fungus stimulates rapid, disordered cell growth, producing the puffy distorted leaves. Spores survive winter on bark and inside dormant buds. Cool wet springs make outbreaks worst; warm dry weather suppresses the fungus.

Severely infected trees lose much of their first flush of leaves. Healthy second flushes usually grow back uninfected.

L19.name: Shot hole disease
L19.shortDescription: Brown spots that dry and fall out, leaving small holes
L19.symptomAnalysis:
Caused by a combination of fungus (*Wilsonomyces carpophilus*) and bacteria. Common on stone fruit trees — cherry, peach, apricot, plum — and on laurels and some ornamentals. Thrives in hot, humid weather.

Damage is mostly cosmetic but can cause significant defoliation in bad years. Doesn't kill plants directly but weakens them.

L20.name: Powdery mildew
L20.shortDescription: White powder coating on leaf surface, wipes off
L20.symptomAnalysis:
A family of fungi that affects most plants. Unusually for a fungus, it thrives in warm, dry conditions with humid nights — opposite of most diseases. Mid-to-late summer is peak season. The white powder is the fungus growing on the surface, not penetrating leaves.

Rarely kills the host but reduces vigor and yield. Severe cases distort leaves and fruit.

For powdery mildew on fruit see [FR06](../04_fruits/powdery-mildew-fruit.md).

L21.name: Sooty mold
L21.shortDescription: Black soot-like coating on leaves under sap-suckers
L21.symptomAnalysis:
Sooty mold is caused by airborne fungal spores landing on the sticky honeydew secreted by sap-sucking insects — aphids, whiteflies, scale, mealybugs. The fungus feeds on the honeydew, not the plant. It's harmless to the plant directly, but the black coating blocks sunlight and reduces photosynthesis.

The real problem isn't the mold; it's the insect infestation that creates the honeydew. Treat the pests, the mold disappears with the next rain.

steps.title: **Identify and treat the underlying pest** — see the relevant entry in [02_pests/](../02_pests/):
steps.body: - Aphids → [P02](../02_pests/aphids.md) - Whiteflies → [P15](../02_pests/whiteflies.md) - Scale → [P03](../02_pests/scale-insects.md) - Mealybugs → [P04](../02_pests/mealybugs.md)

steps.title: Wipe leaves with a damp soapy cloth to remove the visible mold
steps.body: For mealybugs, scale crawlers, or aphids, gently wipe leaves and stems with a soft cloth dipped in soapy water. Re-rinse the cloth often. Avoid bruising tender tissue.

steps.title: A strong water spray rinses off both pests and residue
steps.body: Use a hose nozzle on a stream setting to blast aphids, mites, mealybugs, or lace bugs off leaves and stems. Aim under leaves too. Repeat every few days.

steps.title: Treat sap-suckers as soon as they appear
steps.body: The mold is a downstream symptom.

steps.title: Don't over-fertilize
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

steps.title: Encourage natural predators
steps.body: Buy ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps, or predatory mites from a garden supplier. Release in the evening near the affected plant. Provide nectar plants nearby to keep them around.

steps.title: Stop ants on the trunks of trees with sticky tape — they protect honeydew-producing pests from predators

L22.name: Rust
L22.shortDescription: Orange or reddish-brown raised pustules on leaves
L22.symptomAnalysis:
Rust is caused by thousands of different fungal species in the *Pucciniales* order. They thrive in warm, wet conditions. Spores spread on wind and water. Most rusts have complex life cycles involving two host plants.

Rarely kills, but weakens plants and reduces yield. Common on roses, hollyhocks, snapdragons, beans, daylilies, junipers, apples, pears.

steps.title: Prune off rusted leaves
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Apply copper fungicide weekly
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

steps.title: Stop overhead watering immediately
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Buy rust-resistant varieties
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

steps.title: Inspect new plants for orange spots
steps.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

steps.title: Drip irrigation in the morning
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Wide spacing
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Mulch heavily
steps.body: Spread a 2–3 inch layer of bark, straw, or leaf mulch around the plant, keeping a small ring clear from the stem. Mulch blocks splash-up of soil pathogens and stabilizes moisture.

steps.title: Clear debris in fall
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

L23.name: Gray mold (botrytis)
L23.shortDescription: Gray-brown fuzzy mold on damaged or wet leaves
L23.symptomAnalysis:
Caused by *Botrytis cinerea*, a common fungus that prefers cool, humid, still conditions. It feeds on dying and damaged tissue first, then moves into healthy tissue. Spores spread on water splash and wind.

Strawberries, peonies, roses, lettuce, and many ornamentals are common hosts. Often called "noble rot" on grapes used for sweet wines, but on most plants it's destructive.

For botrytis on flowers see [FL05 — Petal blight](../06_flowers/petal-blight.md).

steps.title: Cut affected tissue back to clean wood
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Sterilize between cuts
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Mulch to stop spore splash-up
steps.body: Spread a 2–3 inch layer of bark, straw, or leaf mulch around the plant, keeping a small ring clear from the stem. Mulch blocks splash-up of soil pathogens and stabilizes moisture.

steps.title: Remove all affected tissue
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Clear all debris around the plant
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Apply copper fungicide every 1–2 weeks
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

steps.title: Let the plant dry between waterings
steps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

homeRemedies.title: Cinnamon Powder
homeRemedies.recipe: Plain ground cinnamon from the spice rack. No mixing.
homeRemedies.usage: Dust a thin layer over moldy soil, on freshly cut stems, or around the base of seedlings. Cinnamon is naturally antifungal and rooting-friendly.

homeRemedies.title: Baking Soda + Dish Soap Spray
homeRemedies.recipe: 1 teaspoon baking soda + 1 teaspoon mild dish soap + 1 quart water. Shake to dissolve.
homeRemedies.usage: Spray on leaves at the first sign of mildew, every 7 days. Coat upper and lower leaf surfaces. Spot-test first; some plants get leaf burn.

steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: No overhead water
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Be careful not to wound plants — wounds invite the fungus
Sterilize garden tools
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Preventive copper in early spring on susceptible plants
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

L24.name: Sap-sucking damage
L24.shortDescription: Tiny dense yellow or white stippling spots
L24.symptomAnalysis:
Caused by sap-sucking insects — aphids, whiteflies, leafhoppers, lace bugs, mites, mealybugs, thrips — piercing leaf tissue and sucking out cell contents. Each puncture leaves a tiny pale dot ("stippling"). With many feeders, the dots merge into a bronzed or silvered look.

The leaves don't kill the plant directly, but stippling weakens it and many of these insects also spread plant viruses.

This entry covers the leaf symptom. For the specific pest causing it, see [02_pests/](../02_pests/).

steps.title: **Identify the pest** — flip leaves over and look for tiny insects under leaves or in joints
steps.body: Match to: - Tiny dots + webs → [P05 — Spider mites](../02_pests/spider-mites.md) - Pear-shaped soft bodies clustered → [P02 — Aphids](../02_pests/aphids.md) - White moth-like flies → [P15 — Whiteflies](../02_pests/whiteflies.md) - Lacy-winged with black dots underneath → [P11 — Lace bugs](../02_pests/lace-bugs.md) - Wedge-shaped jumpers → [P12 — Leafhoppers](../02_pests/leafhoppers.md) - Slender silvery-streak makers → [P10 — Thrips](../02_pests/thrips.md)

steps.title: Spray pests off with hose
steps.body: Use a hose nozzle on a stream setting to blast aphids, mites, mealybugs, or lace bugs off leaves and stems. Aim under leaves too. Repeat every few days.

steps.title: Insecticidal soap
steps.body: Dilute 1 tablespoon of mild liquid dish soap (no degreasers) per quart of water in a spray bottle. Coat the undersides and tops of leaves until dripping. Re-spray every 2–3 days for 1–2 weeks.

steps.title: Release ladybugs and lacewings
steps.body: Buy ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps, or predatory mites from a garden supplier. Release in the evening near the affected plant. Provide nectar plants nearby to keep them around.

steps.title: Inspect new plants for stippling and undersides
steps.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

steps.title: Don't over-fertilize with nitrogen
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

steps.title: Maintain habitat for predator insects
steps.body: Buy ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps, or predatory mites from a garden supplier. Release in the evening near the affected plant. Provide nectar plants nearby to keep them around.

steps.title: Clear weeds and debris
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

P01.name: Caterpillars
P01.shortDescription: Wormlike chewing larvae of moths and butterflies
P01.symptomAnalysis:
Caterpillars chew irregular holes in leaves and can strip foliage to bare veins. Some specialize on certain plants (cabbage white on brassicas, hornworm on tomatoes). Most active in spring and summer; some species feed in fall and winter.

You may see chewed leaves, dark fecal pellets ("frass"), and the caterpillars themselves on undersides of leaves.

steps.title: Hand-pick caterpillars into soapy water
steps.body: Wear gloves. Pick caterpillars, beetles, slugs, or egg clusters off by hand and drop them into a bucket of soapy water. Drown for at least 24 hours, then dispose.

steps.title: Prune heavily damaged leaves
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Apply Bt
steps.body: Spray Bt on leaves and stems where caterpillars feed. Caterpillars must ingest it. Bt is harmless to bees, pets, and humans. Reapply after rain.

steps.title: Release parasitic wasps
steps.body: Buy ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps, or predatory mites from a garden supplier. Release in the evening near the affected plant. Provide nectar plants nearby to keep them around.

homeRemedies.title: Chili Pepper Spray
homeRemedies.recipe: 2 tablespoons cayenne or crushed dried chili in 1 quart of water. Boil 10 minutes. Cool. Strain. Add 1 tsp dish soap.
homeRemedies.usage: Spray on leaves where caterpillars or beetles are feeding. Wear gloves and eye protection while spraying.

steps.title: Cover plants with netting before adults arrive
steps.body: Drape fine mesh netting or floating row covers over plants and secure the edges with stones or pins. Keeps moths, beetles, and caterpillars from laying eggs.

steps.title: Check leaf undersides regularly for clusters of small eggs
Encourage parasitic wasps and birds
steps.body: Buy ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps, or predatory mites from a garden supplier. Release in the evening near the affected plant. Provide nectar plants nearby to keep them around.

steps.title: Clear debris where pupae overwinter
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

P02.name: Aphids
P02.shortDescription: Soft pear-shaped sap-suckers, often green or black
P02.symptomAnalysis:
Aphids pierce plant tissue and suck sap. Heavy infestations wilt new growth, distort leaves, and stunt the plant. They excrete sticky honeydew that drips on lower leaves and grows black [sooty mold](../01_leaves/sooty-mold.md). Heavy ant activity is a tell — ants farm aphids for honeydew.

Aphids reproduce extremely fast (live births, no eggs needed in the growing season). A small cluster becomes an infestation in days. They also spread plant viruses.

steps.title: Blast off with a strong stream of water
steps.body: Use a hose nozzle on a stream setting to blast aphids, mites, mealybugs, or lace bugs off leaves and stems. Aim under leaves too. Repeat every few days.

steps.title: Wipe clusters off with a damp soapy cloth
steps.body: For mealybugs, scale crawlers, or aphids, gently wipe leaves and stems with a soft cloth dipped in soapy water. Re-rinse the cloth often. Avoid bruising tender tissue.

steps.title: Insecticidal soap on undersides of leaves
steps.body: Dilute 1 tablespoon of mild liquid dish soap (no degreasers) per quart of water in a spray bottle. Coat the undersides and tops of leaves until dripping. Re-spray every 2–3 days for 1–2 weeks.

steps.title: Neem oil for stubborn infestations
steps.body: Mix cold-pressed neem oil with water and a few drops of dish soap as an emulsifier (typical 2 tsp neem + 1 tsp soap per quart). Spray to runoff in the cool of evening.

steps.title: Release ladybugs and lacewings
steps.body: Buy ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps, or predatory mites from a garden supplier. Release in the evening near the affected plant. Provide nectar plants nearby to keep them around.

steps.title: Cut off heavily infested tips
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

homeRemedies.title: Garlic Spray
homeRemedies.recipe: Crush 4 garlic cloves into 1 quart of water. Steep overnight. Strain. Add 1 tsp dish soap.
homeRemedies.usage: Spray on leaves and stems, focusing on undersides. Repeat every 5–7 days. Strong-smelling for a day, then dissipates.

homeRemedies.title: Plain Soapy Water Spray
homeRemedies.recipe: 1 teaspoon mild liquid dish soap (no degreasers, no additives) per quart of water in a spray bottle.
homeRemedies.usage: Spray to drench all surfaces, especially leaf undersides, in the evening. Rinse with plain water the next morning. Re-spray every 3 days for 2 weeks.

steps.title: Quarantine and inspect new plants
steps.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

steps.title: Clear weeds nearby
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Maintain habitat for ladybugs and lacewings
steps.body: Buy ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps, or predatory mites from a garden supplier. Release in the evening near the affected plant. Provide nectar plants nearby to keep them around.

steps.title: Don't over-fertilize with nitrogen — soft new growth attracts aphids
Inspect new growth often; catch them early

P03.name: Scale insects
P03.shortDescription: Hard or soft 2–3 mm bumps stuck on leaves and stems
P03.symptomAnalysis:
Scale insects feed on sap. Once attached, they don't move for life. Their hard or soft waxy shell protects them from sprays and predators. Heavy infestations cause leaf yellowing, drop, and dieback.

The crawler stage (newly hatched, before they anchor) is the only mobile stage and the only stage easy to kill with sprays. Sticky honeydew may be present, leading to secondary [sooty mold](../01_leaves/sooty-mold.md).

steps.title: Wipe scales off with a damp cloth
steps.body: For mealybugs, scale crawlers, or aphids, gently wipe leaves and stems with a soft cloth dipped in soapy water. Re-rinse the cloth often. Avoid bruising tender tissue.

steps.title: Trim heavily infested branches
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Neem oil
steps.body: Mix cold-pressed neem oil with water and a few drops of dish soap as an emulsifier (typical 2 tsp neem + 1 tsp soap per quart). Spray to runoff in the cool of evening.

steps.title: Systemic insecticide for crawlers
steps.body: For stubborn or hidden pests, drench the soil with a systemic (imidacloprid, dinotefuran) per label. The plant absorbs it through roots, making sap toxic to sucking insects. Avoid on flowering plants visited by bees.

steps.title: Ladybugs feed on scale outdoors
steps.body: Buy ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps, or predatory mites from a garden supplier. Release in the evening near the affected plant. Provide nectar plants nearby to keep them around.

homeRemedies.title: Rubbing Alcohol Cotton Swab
homeRemedies.recipe: 70% isopropyl rubbing alcohol straight from the bottle.
homeRemedies.usage: Dip a cotton swab and dab directly on individual mealybugs or scale insects. Kills on contact. Avoid soaking the leaf.

steps.title: Keep plants healthy

steps.title: Inspect indoor plants monthly, focusing on leaf undersides and stem joints

P04.name: Mealybugs
P04.shortDescription: Soft white powdery 1–2 mm bugs in leaf joints
P04.symptomAnalysis:
Mealybugs are scale-related sap-suckers covered in a white waxy coating that repels water and many sprays. They live in clusters and hide in protected spots — leaf joints, soil surface, stem cracks. Honeydew leads to [sooty mold](../01_leaves/sooty-mold.md).

A common indoor-plant pest, especially on succulents, orchids, and tropicals.

P05.name: Spider mites
P05.shortDescription: Tiny red or yellow mites, fine webbing
P05.symptomAnalysis:
Spider mites (technically arachnids) are barely visible — about 1/50 inch. They feed on the underside of leaves, sucking out cell contents and leaving tiny pale dots called "stippling". Heavy infestations spin webbing on undersides and across leaf joints.

They thrive in hot, dry, dusty conditions and reproduce explosively (a generation every 5–7 days in warm weather). Indoors, they're most active in winter when home heating dries the air.

P06.name: Leaf beetles
P06.shortDescription: Oval beetles 1–20 mm chewing irregular holes
P06.symptomAnalysis:
A broad family (*Chrysomelidae*) of beetles that chew leaves. They affect most garden plants and can defoliate quickly. Many also feed on flowers, stems, and roots. Most active in spring through fall. They overwinter as adults under bark, leaves, and debris.

Some species (cucumber beetles, Colorado potato beetle, flea beetles) are major garden pests; others cause only minor cosmetic damage.

steps.title: Hand-pick into soapy water (afternoon, when most active)
steps.body: Wear gloves. Pick caterpillars, beetles, slugs, or egg clusters off by hand and drop them into a bucket of soapy water. Drown for at least 24 hours, then dispose.

steps.title: Pyrethrin in evening to protect bees
steps.body: Use a pyrethrin or pyrethrum (chrysanthemum-derived) insecticide per label. Spray in the evening to protect bees. Effective on thrips, leafhoppers, and beetles.

steps.title: Encourage birds and wasps with diverse plantings

steps.title: Row covers from planting until flowering
steps.body: Drape fine mesh netting or floating row covers over plants and secure the edges with stones or pins. Keeps moths, beetles, and caterpillars from laying eggs.

steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Encourage natural predators with diverse plantings
steps.body: Buy ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps, or predatory mites from a garden supplier. Release in the evening near the affected plant. Provide nectar plants nearby to keep them around.

steps.title: Crop rotation breaks beetle life cycles
steps.body: Do not plant the same species — or its botanical relatives — in the same spot two years in a row. A 3-year rotation breaks the lifecycle of soil-borne diseases and pests.

P07.name: Leaf weevils
P07.shortDescription: Heavily armored beetles with chewed leaf margins
P07.symptomAnalysis:
The pale green weevil (*Polydrusus impressifrons*) and similar species feed on leaf edges of hardwood trees — birch, maple, willow, poplar, fruit trees. Damage shows in late spring and early summer. Newly-hatched larvae feed on young roots near the soil line.

Adult weevils have a short feeding period (a few weeks), so mature trees usually shrug off the damage. Young trees and saplings are at higher risk.

P08.name: Longhorn beetles
P08.shortDescription: Long-antennae beetles boring into woody trunks
P08.symptomAnalysis:
Longhorn beetles (*Cerambycidae* family) are wood borers. Adults lay eggs on bark; larvae hatch and tunnel into the trunk to feed on living wood for 1–3 years. Tunneling cuts off the tree's water and nutrient flow. Visible signs: small round exit holes, sawdust at the base of the trunk, branches dying from the top.

They prefer wounded, stressed, or freshly cut hardwoods. Some Asian species are introduced pests of major concern (Asian longhorn beetle, citrus longhorn beetle).

steps.title: Apply a systemic insecticide to high-value trees per label
steps.body: For stubborn or hidden pests, drench the soil with a systemic (imidacloprid, dinotefuran) per label. The plant absorbs it through roots, making sap toxic to sucking insects. Avoid on flowering plants visited by bees.

steps.title: Prune and burn heavily infested branches in dry weather
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Report Asian longhorn beetle sightings to your local agriculture extension — it's a regulated invasive in the US
Keep trees healthy: water, fertilize, and avoid wounds
steps.body: Stressed trees attract beetles.

steps.title: Don't move firewood across regions — common way Asian longhorn spreads
Inspect new trees for boring holes or sawdust
steps.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

steps.title: Prune dead and dying wood promptly so beetles don't establish

P09.name: Slugs and snails
P09.shortDescription: Soft mollusks chewing leaves at night
P09.symptomAnalysis:
Slugs and snails feed mostly at night and on cloudy or rainy days, hiding under boards, rocks, and dense foliage during the day. They favor humid conditions and can devour seedlings entirely. Eggs are laid in clusters in moist soil.

A small number is fine; a population can decimate a vegetable garden or hosta bed in a week.

steps.title: Hand-pick at night with a flashlight
steps.body: Wear gloves. Pick caterpillars, beetles, slugs, or egg clusters off by hand and drop them into a bucket of soapy water. Drown for at least 24 hours, then dispose.

steps.title: Clear hiding spots — boards, stones, dense weed cover
Place small boards in the garden as traps
steps.body: Lift them in the morning to find slugs hiding underneath.

steps.title: Iron phosphate slug bait (sold as Sluggo, Escar-Go) is safer for pets and birds than metaldehyde-based baits
steps.body: Spread around plants in evening.

homeRemedies.title: Coffee Grounds Barrier
homeRemedies.recipe: Used coffee grounds dried out on a tray.
homeRemedies.usage: Sprinkle a 1/2 inch ring around the base of slug-prone plants. Rough texture and caffeine repel slugs and snails. Replace after rain.

homeRemedies.title: Crushed Eggshell
homeRemedies.recipe: Rinsed eggshells, dried, crushed coarsely with a rolling pin.
homeRemedies.usage: (a) Mix into soil at planting for slow calcium release — helps prevent blossom-end rot. (b) Sprinkle a sharp-edged ring around plants — slugs avoid the edges.

homeRemedies.title: Beer Trap
homeRemedies.recipe: Bury a shallow dish or cup with the rim level with the soil. Fill 2/3 with cheap beer. (Or 1 cup water + 1 tsp dry yeast + 1 tsp sugar.)
homeRemedies.usage: Place near affected plants in the evening. Slugs and snails crawl in and drown. Refresh every 2–3 days.

steps.title: Eliminate hiding spots
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Copper tape forms a barrier slugs won't cross — wrap pots and raised beds
Water in the morning so plants are dry by evening
Encourage natural predators (toads, ducks, ground beetles)
steps.body: Buy ladybugs, lacewings, parasitic wasps, or predatory mites from a garden supplier. Release in the evening near the affected plant. Provide nectar plants nearby to keep them around.

P10.name: Thrips
P10.shortDescription: Tiny slender 1–2 mm fast-moving silver-streak makers
P10.symptomAnalysis:
Thrips have rasping mouthparts that scrape plant cells and suck out the contents, leaving silvery scars and a pale stippled look. They hide in flower buds and leaf joints. Heavy infestations distort leaves and damage developing flowers and fruits.

Thrips are vectors for several plant viruses, so even a small population can cause secondary disease.

steps.title: Strong water spray
steps.body: Use a hose nozzle on a stream setting to blast aphids, mites, mealybugs, or lace bugs off leaves and stems. Aim under leaves too. Repeat every few days.

steps.title: Yellow or blue sticky traps catch flying adults
Pyrethrin spray
steps.body: Use a pyrethrin or pyrethrum (chrysanthemum-derived) insecticide per label. Spray in the evening to protect bees. Effective on thrips, leafhoppers, and beetles.

steps.title: Insecticidal soap on undersides of leaves
steps.body: Dilute 1 tablespoon of mild liquid dish soap (no degreasers) per quart of water in a spray bottle. Coat the undersides and tops of leaves until dripping. Re-spray every 2–3 days for 1–2 weeks.

steps.title: Release minute pirate bugs and lacewings

homeRemedies.recipe: 1 teaspoon mild liquid dish soap (no degreasers, no additives) per quart of water in a spray bottle.
homeRemedies.usage: Spray to drench all surfaces, especially leaf undersides, in the evening. Rinse with plain water the next morning. Re-spray every 3 days for 2 weeks.

P11.name: Lace bugs
P11.shortDescription: Lace-winged sap-suckers, white stippling on leaves
P11.symptomAnalysis:
Lace bugs use needle-like mouthparts to suck nutrients from the underside of leaves, causing pale stippling on the upper surface. They leave clusters of black tar-like droppings on the leaf underside. Most common in mid- to late-summer at peak population.

Damage is mostly cosmetic, but severe infestations weaken plants and leave them open to other pests and diseases. Common targets: azaleas, rhododendrons, sycamores, oaks, lantana.

P12.name: Leafhoppers
P12.shortDescription: Wedge-shaped jumpers, light speckling on leaves
P12.symptomAnalysis:
Leafhoppers feed on plant sap from leaves, stems, and stems of new growth. Heavy feeding causes leaf curl, yellowing ("hopper burn"), and stunted growth. They jump or fly off when disturbed. Common hosts: beans, corn, lettuce, beets, potatoes, grapes, roses.

Like thrips, leafhoppers transmit viral and bacterial diseases between plants — sometimes more harmful than the insect itself.

steps.body: Use a pyrethrin or pyrethrum (chrysanthemum-derived) insecticide per label. Spray in the evening to protect bees. Effective on thrips, leafhoppers, and beetles.

steps.body: Drape fine mesh netting or floating row covers over plants and secure the edges with stones or pins. Keeps moths, beetles, and caterpillars from laying eggs.

P13.name: Tortrix moths
P13.shortDescription: Caterpillars rolling leaves into silken tubes
P13.symptomAnalysis:
Tortrix (leaf-roller) moth caterpillars roll leaves around themselves and tie them shut with silk, then feed from inside the protected tube. They also feed on fruit, sometimes webbing fruits to leaves. Many species; found on fruits, vegetables, and ornamentals. Most active late spring through early fall.

Greenhouse populations can be year-round.

steps.title: Prune off rolled leaves

steps.title: Pheromone traps catch males and disrupt breeding
steps.body: Hang species-specific pheromone traps in or near affected plants. Traps catch male moths, suppressing the next generation. Replace lures every 4–6 weeks during the season.

steps.title: Apply Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt)
steps.body: Spray Bt on leaves and stems where caterpillars feed. Caterpillars must ingest it. Bt is harmless to bees, pets, and humans. Reapply after rain.

P14.name: Leaf miners
P14.shortDescription: Fly larvae tunneling white squiggles inside leaves
P14.symptomAnalysis:
Leaf miners are tiny fly, moth, or beetle larvae that tunnel between the upper and lower layers of a leaf, eating the soft tissue inside. The visible trails are the empty tunnels they leave behind. The adult fly lays eggs between leaf layers; hatched larvae feed for 1–3 weeks then drop out to pupate in soil.

Cosmetic damage on most ornamentals; significant damage on edible greens (spinach, chard, beets).

steps.title: Pinch out leaves with active mines and discard
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Neem oil
steps.body: Mix cold-pressed neem oil with water and a few drops of dish soap as an emulsifier (typical 2 tsp neem + 1 tsp soap per quart). Spray to runoff in the cool of evening.

P15.name: Whiteflies
P15.shortDescription: Tiny white moth-like flies under leaves, honeydew
P15.symptomAnalysis:
Whiteflies are sap-suckers like aphids and mealybugs, with the same effects: yellowing leaves, distorted growth, honeydew, and follow-up [sooty mold](../01_leaves/sooty-mold.md). They are common on tomatoes, peppers, eggplants, hibiscus, and many indoor plants.

Each adult lays hundreds of eggs on leaf undersides. Nymphs are flat oval scales, almost translucent, glued to the leaf. Heavy infestations multiply explosively in warm weather.

P16.name: Fungus gnats (adults)
P16.shortDescription: Black gnats hovering near houseplant soil
P16.symptomAnalysis:
Adult fungus gnats are small (1/8 inch), black, mosquito-like flies. They are weak fliers and tend to walk on soil surfaces. Adults don't damage plants directly — but each female lays ~200 eggs in moist soil, and the larvae feed on tender root hairs. See [R07](../05_roots/fungus-gnat-larvae.md) for the larval/root damage.

Common in any indoor plant kept too wet, especially seedlings, ferns, and tropicals.

steps.title: Let topsoil dry between waterings
steps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

steps.title: Bottom-water only
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Top-dress with sand to block egg-laying
steps.body: Scrape away the top 1/8 inch of moldy or salt-crusted soil and replace it with a 1/4 inch layer of clean coarse sand or fine gravel. Water sparingly afterward.

steps.title: Yellow sticky traps placed flat or angled at soil level catch adults
Bt-i (*Bacillus thuringiensis israelensis*, sold as "Mosquito Bits") sprinkled on soil or steeped in water kills larvae

homeRemedies.title: Apple Cider Vinegar Trap
homeRemedies.recipe: Pour 1/4 cup apple cider vinegar into a small yellow cup or saucer. Add 1 drop of dish soap to break surface tension. Cover with plastic wrap and poke small holes.
homeRemedies.usage: Place near the affected plant. Adult fungus gnats and fruit flies are attracted, fall in, and drown. Refresh every 3–4 days.

steps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

ST01.name: Wilting
ST01.symptomAnalysis:
Wilting can come from any of:

- **Water stress** — too much or too little. Drought wilts dry; overwatering rots roots and they can no longer pull water up.
- **Heat stress** — hot afternoons cause temporary wilting that recovers overnight.
- **Vascular wilt disease** — *Verticillium*, *Fusarium*, or bacterial wilt clog the plant's water-carrying tissue. Leaves wilt and stay wilted.
- **Root rot** — see [R01](../05_roots/root-rot.md). Damaged roots can't deliver water.

To narrow it down: scratch the main stem with a fingernail. Green inside means alive; brown and brittle means dead. Then check the soil and the roots.

steps.title: Diagnose first
steps.body: Soil dry → *Water Deeply But Less Often* (see 1.1). Soil wet → *Reduce Watering Frequency* (see 1.2) and check roots.

subSteps.title: Water Deeply But Less Often
subSteps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

subSteps.title: Reduce Watering Frequency
subSteps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

steps.title: Use shade cloth on hot afternoons
steps.body: Relocate the plant to a north-facing window, under a tree, or behind shade cloth. Sunburn and leaf scorch usually heal once the plant cools off.

steps.title: For disease, prune and destroy affected branches
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Sterilize tools between cuts
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting. If diagnosed with vascular wilt disease: *Discard the Severely Infected Plant* (see 4.1). Soil pathogens linger for years.

subSteps.title: Discard the Severely Infected Plant
subSteps.body: When more than half the plant is affected or root rot is widespread, dig the plant up, seal it in a heavy trash bag, and dispose. Sterilize the area before replanting.

steps.title: Water consistently and deeply
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: Buy wilt-resistant varieties (look for "VFN" on tomatoes, strawberries)
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

steps.title: Rotate beds
steps.body: Do not plant the same species — or its botanical relatives — in the same spot two years in a row. A 3-year rotation breaks the lifecycle of soil-borne diseases and pests.

steps.title: Mulch to keep moisture even
steps.body: Spread a 2–3 inch layer of bark, straw, or leaf mulch around the plant, keeping a small ring clear from the stem. Mulch blocks splash-up of soil pathogens and stabilizes moisture.

ST02.name: Twig blight
ST02.shortDescription: Whole twigs turn brown and dry from base
ST02.symptomAnalysis:
A fungal disease common in nursery-grown plants that were spaced too closely together. Spores enter through wounds or in wet weather. Unlike dieback (which starts at the tip), twig blight kills the whole infected twig at once. Sunken dead patches called *cankers* may form between living and dead wood.

For dieback see [ST03](dieback.md). For cankers see [ST05](cankers.md).

steps.title: Cut affected twigs back to healthy wood, in dry weather
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Sterilize between every cut
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Apply copper fungicide on remaining healthy growth
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

steps.title: Rake up dropped material
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Buy resistant varieties from reputable nurseries
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

steps.title: Inspect new plants for any dead twigs
steps.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

steps.title: Plant with proper spacing for airflow
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Drip irrigation
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Preventive copper spray in damp springs
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

ST03.name: Dieback
ST03.shortDescription: Stems die back from the tip toward the trunk
ST03.symptomAnalysis:
A general term for fungal infections (and sometimes insect or environmental damage) that kill woody growth from the tip inward. Often follows wounds — pruning cuts, mower damage, frost cracks, insect tunnels — that gave fungi a way in. Stress (drought, freeze, waterlogging) makes plants more vulnerable.

Distinguishes from twig blight: dieback starts at the tip and works back; twig blight kills the whole twig at once.

steps.title: Prune Infected Leaves and Stems
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Sterilize between cuts
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Switch to base watering
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: A nitrogen-rich feed gives stressed plants energy to recover
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative. In severe whole-plant cases: *Discard the Severely Infected Plant* (see 4.1).

subSteps.title: Discard the Severely Infected Plant
subSteps.body: When more than half the plant is affected or root rot is widespread, dig the plant up, seal it in a heavy trash bag, and dispose. Sterilize the area before replanting.

steps.title: Plant in the right place — appropriate light, drainage, and exposure for the species
Protect plants from physical damage (mowers, weed whips, animal browsing)
Maintain airflow with proper spacing
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Prune only in dry weather to avoid letting fungi into fresh cuts
Inspect after cold or storms
