ST04.name: Stem rot (soft rot)
ST04.shortDescription: Stem turns mushy, hollow, and foul-smelling
ST04.symptomAnalysis:
Bacterial soft rot, often *Erwinia*. Bacteria enter through wounds and digest plant tissue from the inside. Develops fastest in warm, humid weather and on plants standing in wet soil or splashed by overhead watering. Once advanced, it spreads from stem to crown and down into the roots — sometimes the whole plant goes black and mushy from the center.

Includes the "whole plant turning black from the center" presentation that some references list as a separate "soft rot" condition.

groups.label: For mild infection (one stem or section affected)

steps.title: Cut out every soft, discolored stem section into clean tissue
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Sterilize between every cut
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Repot in fresh sterile soil with drainage
steps.body: Move the plant to a clean pot with drainage holes, using fresh sterile potting mix. Bury roots up to the same depth as before. Water lightly to settle the soil.

steps.title: Water only when topsoil is dry
steps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

steps.title: Stop overhead watering
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread. For severe whole-plant infection: *Discard the Severely Infected Plant* (see 5.1). Bag and remove. Don't compost.

subSteps.title: Discard the Severely Infected Plant
subSteps.body: When more than half the plant is affected or root rot is widespread, dig the plant up, seal it in a heavy trash bag, and dispose. Sterilize the area before replanting.

steps.title: Avoid wetting foliage and stems
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Don't overwater
steps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

steps.title: Space plants apart
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Inspect new plants for soft, discolored stems
steps.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

steps.title: Always sterilize tools
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Control sap-sucking pests like aphids; their wounds invite bacteria

ST05.name: Cankers
ST05.shortDescription: Sunken, dead bark patches with cracked margins
ST05.symptomAnalysis:
A canker is a localized dead zone in stem tissue caused by fungi (*Cytospora*, *Nectria*) or bacteria entering through a wound — pruning cuts, frost cracks, sunscald, insect tunnels. The pathogen kills tissue around the entry point. Cankers grow slowly; large ones can girdle and kill an entire branch.

Common on fruit trees (peach, apple, cherry) and many ornamentals. Often follows winter stress.

steps.title: Prune Infected Leaves and Stems
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Sterilize between every cut
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Prune only in dry weather to avoid spreading the pathogen
Apply copper fungicide to remaining healthy bark in early spring
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days. For cankers on the main trunk that you can't prune off: scrape out dead tissue with a clean knife back to healthy bark (called "surgery"). Leave the wound exposed to air to dry.

steps.title: Avoid winter and frost damage — wrap young trunks, paint with white tree paint, or use tree wraps in cold zones
Avoid mechanical wounds — keep mowers and trimmers away from trunks
Pick canker-resistant cultivars
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

steps.title: Keep plants healthy
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

steps.title: Prune in dry late-winter or early-spring weather

ST06.name: Sunscald on bark
ST06.shortDescription: Cracked, peeling, discolored bark on the sun-facing side
ST06.symptomAnalysis:
Sunscald happens when intense winter sun warms one side of a trunk during the day, the cells become active, and then a sudden temperature drop at night kills them. Young, smooth-barked trees (maple, fruit trees, recently transplanted ornamentals) are most vulnerable. Damaged bark becomes a long-term entry point for pathogens — see [ST05 — Cankers](cankers.md).

It can also occur in summer heat after a sudden defoliation that exposes previously shaded bark.

steps.title: Do not remove damaged bark unless it is loose and lifting away
steps.body: Most sunscald wounds heal slowly on their own.

steps.title: Trim away any clearly dead, peeling bark with a sterile knife to the edge of healthy tissue
Apply copper fungicide to keep pathogens out of the wound
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

steps.title: Watch for cankers developing — see [ST05](cankers.md)
**Wrap young tree trunks** with white tree wrap or paint with diluted white interior latex paint (1:1 with water) from the soil line up 24 inches, applied in late fall
steps.body: Reflects winter sun.

steps.title: Avoid late-summer pruning that exposes previously shaded bark
Water deeply in late fall before the ground freezes
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: Mulch the root zone to moderate temperature swings
steps.body: Spread a 2–3 inch layer of bark, straw, or leaf mulch around the plant, keeping a small ring clear from the stem. Mulch blocks splash-up of soil pathogens and stabilizes moisture.

ST07.name: Witches' broom
ST07.shortDescription: Dense brush-like cluster of stunted shoots from one point
ST07.symptomAnalysis:
A stress response, not a single disease. Many causes: rust fungi, insect feeding, bacterial or fungal infection, infestation by parasitic plants like dwarf mistletoe. The plant responds by producing many short shoots in a tight cluster instead of normal extension growth.

The broom itself rarely kills the plant, but the underlying cause may. Common on conifers, fruit trees, blackberries, and many woody shrubs.

steps.title: Cut the broom out, pruning several inches below the affected point
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Sterilize tools between cuts
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Determine the cause:
steps.body: - Rust fungus → *Apply Copper Fungicide* (see 3.1) - Mites or insects → see [02_pests/](../02_pests/) - Mistletoe → physically remove the parasite

subSteps.title: Apply Copper Fungicide
subSteps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

steps.title: Clear pruned material away from the plant
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Monitor regularly for pest and disease pressure
Preventive copper spray on rust-prone species
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

steps.title: Prune out stressed or weak branches early
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: In bad infestations, remove the entire plant
steps.body: When more than half the plant is affected or root rot is widespread, dig the plant up, seal it in a heavy trash bag, and dispose. Sterilize the area before replanting.

ST08.name: Crown gall
ST08.shortDescription: Rough tumor-like growths near the soil line
ST08.symptomAnalysis:
Caused by the bacterium *Agrobacterium tumefaciens*, which lives in soil and enters through wounds. Affects over 600 plant species — roses, fruit trees, grapes, raspberries are particularly susceptible. The bacterium hijacks the plant's DNA and causes uncontrolled tissue growth.

Established trees usually survive crown gall. Young plants and severely infected ones decline because the gall blocks water and nutrient flow.

steps.title: On established trees, cut out the gall with a sterile knife or pruners
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Sterilize between every cut
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: After surgery, paint the wound with a tree pruning sealer
For young or heavily infected plants: [S43](../shared/treatment-steps.md#s43-discard-the-severely-infected-plant) — Remove and destroy
steps.body: Do not compost.

steps.title: Solarize the soil before replanting
steps.body: Lay a clear plastic sheet over moist garden soil for 4–6 weeks during the hottest part of the year. Heat builds underneath and kills nematodes, fungi, and weed seeds.

steps.title: Inspect every new plant for galls at the crown
steps.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

steps.title: Always sterilize tools
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Don't pile soil or mulch up against the crown of plants — keeps the area dry and pathogen-free
Plant resistant cultivars where available
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

steps.title: Use a beneficial bacterial dip (Agrobacterium radiobacter strain 84, sold as "Galltrol") on bare-root stock at planting

ST09.name: Stem scars
ST09.shortDescription: Healed scrapes, gouges, or pest damage on stems
ST09.symptomAnalysis:
Scars form when a plant heals a wound. Common causes:

- **Mechanical damage** — pets, kids, mowers, garden tools, foot traffic.
- **Pest damage left behind** — feeding sites from insects that have already moved on.
- **Sunburn / sunscald** — heat damage on exposed bark. See [ST06](sunscald-bark.md).
- **Lost leaves or buds** — dropped leaves leave a scar on the stem.

Scars themselves don't damage the plant beyond the original wound. They are mostly cosmetic. But the damaged area is a permanent entry point for future pests and pathogens.

steps.title: Address the underlying cause:
steps.body: - Pets/kids/tools → install a barrier or move plant. - Pests → identify the insect and treat. See [02_pests/](../02_pests/). - Sunburn → *Move to a Shaded Location* (see 1.1) Move out of harsh sun. - Frequent leaf loss → check light, water, and nutrients.

subSteps.title: Move to a Shaded Location
subSteps.body: Relocate the plant to a north-facing window, under a tree, or behind shade cloth. Sunburn and leaf scorch usually heal once the plant cools off.

steps.title: The scar itself does not need treatment
Watch for cankers developing on damaged areas — see [ST05](cankers.md)
Set up barriers around plants in busy areas
Keep mowers and weed whips clear of trunks
Use sterile pots and fresh soil for new plants to avoid pest transfer
steps.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

steps.title: Inspect plants regularly for early signs of damage

FR01.name: Fruit spot
FR01.shortDescription: Brown or black streaks and lesions on fruit skin
FR01.symptomAnalysis: Fruit spot is a catch-all term for bacterial and fungal infections that leave dark lesions on the fruit. Pathogens travel by wind, rain splash, insects, and tools. They overwinter in plant debris and re-infect in spring. Wet conditions accelerate spread. Severely spotted fruit may be inedible; mildly spotted fruit usually is fine after cutting away the spot.

steps.title: Remove and destroy badly spotted fruit
steps.body: Pick all spotted, rotted, or moldy fruit from the plant and from the ground beneath. Bury (12 inches deep), burn, or seal in a trash bag — do not compost.

steps.title: Prune infected twigs that may carry the pathogen
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Sterilize tools between cuts
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Apply copper fungicide/bactericide per label
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

steps.title: Buy spot-resistant cultivars
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

steps.title: Rotate annual fruit crops every 3 years
steps.body: Do not plant the same species — or its botanical relatives — in the same spot two years in a row. A 3-year rotation breaks the lifecycle of soil-borne diseases and pests.

steps.title: Clear all fallen fruit and leaves at end of season
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Drip irrigation or base watering only
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Space plants for airflow
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

FR02.name: Fruit rot
FR02.shortDescription: Sunken brown rot, blossom-end blackening, fungal mush
FR02.symptomAnalysis:
Three common patterns:

1. **Blossom-end rot** — sunken black crater at the bottom of tomatoes, peppers, squash. Caused by calcium deficiency in the fruit, not a disease. Inconsistent watering disrupts calcium uptake.
2. **Brown rot (fungal)** — small brown spots that expand and may grow tan-gray fuzzy spore clumps. Affects stone fruits (peach, plum, cherry).
3. **Overripe rot** — fruit left on the plant past ripening, attracting insects and fungi.

steps.title: Establish consistent deep watering
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: Mulch to keep soil moisture even
steps.body: Spread a 2–3 inch layer of bark, straw, or leaf mulch around the plant, keeping a small ring clear from the stem. Mulch blocks splash-up of soil pathogens and stabilizes moisture.

steps.title: Remove affected fruit so the plant redirects calcium to remaining fruit
steps.body: Pick all spotted, rotted, or moldy fruit from the plant and from the ground beneath. Bury (12 inches deep), burn, or seal in a trash bag — do not compost.

steps.title: Remove all visibly rotted fruit, including those on the ground
steps.body: Pick all spotted, rotted, or moldy fruit from the plant and from the ground beneath. Bury (12 inches deep), burn, or seal in a trash bag — do not compost.

steps.title: Prune affected twigs
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Apply copper fungicide on ripening fruit, not green fruit
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

homeRemedies.title: Crushed Eggshell
homeRemedies.recipe: Rinsed eggshells, dried, crushed coarsely with a rolling pin.
homeRemedies.usage: (a) Mix into soil at planting for slow calcium release — helps prevent blossom-end rot. (b) Sprinkle a sharp-edged ring around plants — slugs avoid the edges.

steps.title: Consistent watering is the single biggest factor
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: Mulch
steps.body: Spread a 2–3 inch layer of bark, straw, or leaf mulch around the plant, keeping a small ring clear from the stem. Mulch blocks splash-up of soil pathogens and stabilizes moisture.

steps.title: Use a balanced fertilizer
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

steps.title: Thin fruit and prune for airflow
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Pick fruit promptly when ripe

FR03.name: Fruit deformity
FR03.symptomAnalysis:
Several factors:

- **Poor pollination** — bees missed some ovules, so the fruit develops unevenly. Common: pointy-ended strawberries, lopsided cucumbers.
- **Watering swings** — drought followed by heavy water can cause splitting; uneven moisture causes cat-facing in tomatoes.
- **Temperature extremes** — cold during flowering damages developing fruit.
- **Nutrient deficiencies** — boron and calcium shortages cause stunting and odd shapes.
- **Insect damage at the bud stage** — tarnished plant bugs feeding on flowers leave scars and dimples.

Most deformed fruit is still edible.

steps.title: Remove the worst fruit so the plant focuses on healthy ones
steps.body: Pick all spotted, rotted, or moldy fruit from the plant and from the ground beneath. Bury (12 inches deep), burn, or seal in a trash bag — do not compost.

steps.title: Stabilize watering immediately
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: Apply a balanced fertilizer with micronutrients (boron, calcium)
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

steps.title: Plant pollinator-friendly flowers nearby
steps.body: Hand-pollinate squash and melons with a small paintbrush if you see few bees.

steps.title: Cover flowering plants from late frost
steps.body: Cover vulnerable plants with a frost cloth, burlap, sheet, or row cover before nightfall. Bring potted plants indoors. Remove the cover the next morning.

steps.title: Consistent moisture, especially during fruit set
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: Apply slow-release fertilizer at planting
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

steps.title: Check for insect damage on developing fruit
steps.body: See [02_pests/](../02_pests/).

FR04.name: Fruit withering
FR04.shortDescription: Shriveled mummified fruit hanging on the plant
FR04.symptomAnalysis:
A fungal infection (often *Monilinia* on stone fruits) that overwinters in dried-up fruit and plant debris. Spores splash up in spring and infect new flowers and developing fruit, which then desiccate before ripening. Warm, humid spring/summer weather drives outbreaks.

The mummified fruit you see in winter or late season is the source of next year's infection.

steps.title: Pick every shriveled mummy off the plant and from the ground
steps.body: Pick all spotted, rotted, or moldy fruit from the plant and from the ground beneath. Bury (12 inches deep), burn, or seal in a trash bag — do not compost.

steps.title: Prune any affected twigs
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Spray copper fungicide
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

steps.title: Open up the canopy with selective pruning
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Clear all dropped fruit at end of season
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Stake plants up off the ground
steps.body: Drive a wooden or bamboo stake into the soil near the plant and tie loose figure-eight loops with soft twine. Keeps stems off the wet ground and improves airflow.

steps.title: Prune for airflow during dormant season
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Preventive copper spray at flowering and again pre-harvest
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

FR05.name: Mold on fruit
FR05.shortDescription: Gray, white, or brown fuzzy mold on fruit surface
FR05.symptomAnalysis: A range of fungi cause fuzzy molds on fruit. They thrive in cool, moist, still conditions — early and late season. Even a mild infection ruins the fruit; a severe one wipes out the plant. Often follows blossom-end rot or insect damage that gave fungi an entry point.

steps.title: Remove all moldy fruit, including any on the ground
steps.body: Pick all spotted, rotted, or moldy fruit from the plant and from the ground beneath. Bury (12 inches deep), burn, or seal in a trash bag — do not compost.

steps.title: Prune affected leaves and stems near the moldy fruit
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Trim back surrounding growth for airflow
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Apply copper fungicide on remaining ripening fruit
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

homeRemedies.title: Apple Cider Vinegar Trap
homeRemedies.recipe: Pour 1/4 cup apple cider vinegar into a small yellow cup or saucer. Add 1 drop of dish soap to break surface tension. Cover with plastic wrap and poke small holes.
homeRemedies.usage: Place near the affected plant. Adult fungus gnats and fruit flies are attracted, fall in, and drown. Refresh every 3–4 days.

homeRemedies.title: Aspirin Water Foliar Spray
homeRemedies.recipe: 1 uncoated aspirin tablet (325 mg) dissolved in 1 gallon of water with a few drops of dish soap.
homeRemedies.usage: Spray on leaves every 2–3 weeks. Salicylic acid in aspirin gently stimulates the plant's own disease-defense response.

steps.title: Rake away rotting debris and fallen fruit
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Drip irrigation only
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Don't overcrowd
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Don't over-fertilize early
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

FR06.name: Powdery mildew on fruit
FR06.shortDescription: White powder coating on fruit and stem
FR06.symptomAnalysis:
Powdery mildew fungi prefer warm, dry days and cool, humid nights — opposite of most fungal diseases. Mid- to late-summer outbreaks are typical. The white coating is the fungus growing on the surface; it rarely kills the fruit but reduces yield, ruins appearance, and can spread to the rest of the plant.

For the same fungus on leaves see [L20 — Powdery mildew](../01_leaves/powdery-mildew.md).

steps.title: Trim off heavily mildewed fruit
steps.body: Pick all spotted, rotted, or moldy fruit from the plant and from the ground beneath. Bury (12 inches deep), burn, or seal in a trash bag — do not compost.

steps.title: Cut back nearby mildewed leaves and stems
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Open up the canopy with selective pruning
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Apply copper fungicide for stubborn cases
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

homeRemedies.title: Baking Soda + Dish Soap Spray
homeRemedies.recipe: 1 teaspoon baking soda + 1 teaspoon mild dish soap + 1 quart water. Shake to dissolve.
homeRemedies.usage: Spray on leaves at the first sign of mildew, every 7 days. Coat upper and lower leaf surfaces. Spot-test first; some plants get leaf burn.

homeRemedies.title: Milk Spray
homeRemedies.recipe: 1 part milk (any fat, but whole works best) to 9 parts water in a spray bottle.
homeRemedies.usage: Spray on leaves once a week in bright weather. Sunlight activates milk proteins into a mild antifungal coat.

steps.title: Buy mildew-resistant cultivars (especially squash, cucumbers, melons)
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

steps.title: Drip irrigation, not overhead
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Wide spacing
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Clear debris before winter
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

FR07.name: Fruit cracking
FR07.shortDescription: Splits and cracks across fruit skin
FR07.symptomAnalysis:
Cracking happens when fruit growth speeds up faster than the skin can stretch. The most common trigger is a sudden burst of water — heavy rain or deep watering after a drought. Sweet-thin-skinned varieties are most vulnerable.

Cracked fruit attracts insects and rot fungi quickly, but the fruit itself is usually still edible if picked promptly.

steps.title: Pick cracked fruit immediately
steps.body: Don't wait for full ripeness — they will rot or split further.

steps.title: Discard fruit with mold or insect damage in the cracks
steps.body: Pick all spotted, rotted, or moldy fruit from the plant and from the ground beneath. Bury (12 inches deep), burn, or seal in a trash bag — do not compost.

steps.title: Resume consistent watering immediately
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: Consistent moisture is the single biggest factor
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: Mulch heavily to even out soil moisture
steps.body: Spread a 2–3 inch layer of bark, straw, or leaf mulch around the plant, keeping a small ring clear from the stem. Mulch blocks splash-up of soil pathogens and stabilizes moisture.

steps.title: Buy crack-resistant varieties (e
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

steps.title: Harvest just before peak ripeness if heavy rain is forecast
Stake heavy fruit to avoid stress on the skin
steps.body: Drive a wooden or bamboo stake into the soil near the plant and tie loose figure-eight loops with soft twine. Keeps stems off the wet ground and improves airflow.

R01.name: Root rot
R01.shortDescription: Roots black, mushy, smelly; plant wilted despite wet soil
R01.symptomAnalysis: Healthy roots are white or tan and pliable. Rotted roots are dark and slimy. Two main causes: chronic overwatering starves roots of oxygen and they die back; or a soil-borne fungus (Pythium, Phytophthora) becomes active in wet soil and attacks roots. Once the rot starts, it spreads to healthy roots even after the watering issue is fixed.

steps.title: Unpot and rinse roots so you can see what is healthy
steps.body: Slide the plant out of its pot, gently shake off loose soil, and rinse the root ball under lukewarm running water until you can clearly see individual roots.

steps.title: Sterilize pruners
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Cut away every black or mushy root, back to firm tissue
steps.body: Cut away black, mushy, or shriveled roots with sterilized pruners. Healthy roots are white or tan and pliable. Cut back to clean, firm tissue.

steps.title: Repot in fresh sterile mix in a clean pot with drainage holes
steps.body: Move the plant to a clean pot with drainage holes, using fresh sterile potting mix. Bury roots up to the same depth as before. Water lightly to settle the soil.

steps.title: Prune back about half the leaves to lower the demand on the new root system
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Wait for the topsoil to dry before watering again
steps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil. If more than two-thirds of the roots are gone: *Discard the Severely Infected Plant* (see 6.1).

subSteps.title: Discard the Severely Infected Plant
subSteps.body: When more than half the plant is affected or root rot is widespread, dig the plant up, seal it in a heavy trash bag, and dispose. Sterilize the area before replanting.

homeRemedies.title: Hydrogen Peroxide Soil Drench
homeRemedies.recipe: 1 part 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide to 3 parts water. (Or 1 part 35% food-grade peroxide to 30 parts water.)
homeRemedies.usage: Pour around the base of the plant until the topsoil is moistened. Repeat once a week until the soil dries normally and bubbling stops.

steps.title: Water at the base of the plant
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Let the top inch of soil dry before each watering
steps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

steps.title: Always use pots with drainage holes
steps.body: Move the plant to a clean pot with drainage holes, using fresh sterile potting mix. Bury roots up to the same depth as before. Water lightly to settle the soil.

steps.title: Sterilize pots before reusing them
steps.body: Soak empty pots for 10 minutes in a 1:9 bleach-to-water solution, then rinse thoroughly with clean water and air-dry before refilling with fresh soil.

steps.title: Inspect new plants before integrating them
steps.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

R02.name: Root deformity
R02.symptomAnalysis:
Three usual causes:
1. **Root-knot nematodes** — microscopic worms that burrow into roots and trigger swollen galls.
2. **Bacterial or fungal infection** — produces irregular swellings as a stress response.
3. **Physical conditions** — rocky, compacted, or crowded soil forces roots to twist around obstacles.

Note: small round root *nodules* on legumes (beans, peas) are beneficial nitrogen-fixing bacteria, not damage. Nodules rub off easily; galls do not.

steps.title: Unpot and inspect
steps.body: Slide the plant out of its pot, gently shake off loose soil, and rinse the root ball under lukewarm running water until you can clearly see individual roots.

steps.title: Trim mildly affected roots
steps.body: Cut away black, mushy, or shriveled roots with sterilized pruners. Healthy roots are white or tan and pliable. Cut back to clean, firm tissue.

steps.title: Sterilize tools between cuts
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Replant in fresh, loose, sterile soil
steps.body: Move the plant to a clean pot with drainage holes, using fresh sterile potting mix. Bury roots up to the same depth as before. Water lightly to settle the soil.

steps.title: For garden beds with nematodes, solarize the soil for 4–6 weeks
steps.body: Lay a clear plastic sheet over moist garden soil for 4–6 weeks during the hottest part of the year. Heat builds underneath and kills nematodes, fungi, and weed seeds. If most roots are deformed: *Discard the Severely Infected Plant* (see 5.1).

subSteps.title: Discard the Severely Infected Plant
subSteps.body: When more than half the plant is affected or root rot is widespread, dig the plant up, seal it in a heavy trash bag, and dispose. Sterilize the area before replanting.

steps.title: Choose nematode-resistant varieties (look for "VFN" on tomato seeds)
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

steps.title: Remove old roots and debris
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Rotate crops every 3 years
steps.body: Do not plant the same species — or its botanical relatives — in the same spot two years in a row. A 3-year rotation breaks the lifecycle of soil-borne diseases and pests.

steps.title: Inspect roots of new plants before introducing them
steps.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

steps.title: Keep plants well-fed and well-watered so they resist infection
steps.body: Use an N-P-K balanced fertilizer (e.g. 10-10-10) per label. For deficiencies showing in leaves, follow up with a foliar feed for fast uptake. Compost is a slow-release alternative.

R03.name: Mold on soil
R03.shortDescription: White or yellow fuzz, or mushrooms, on soil surface
R03.symptomAnalysis: Saprophytic fungi feed on dead organic matter in damp soil. They are mostly harmless to plants, but they signal the soil is staying too wet, which sets up bigger problems like root rot or fungus gnats. Mushrooms come from spores that arrived in the potting mix.

steps.title: Scrape off the moldy top layer and replace with coarse sand
steps.body: Scrape away the top 1/8 inch of moldy or salt-crusted soil and replace it with a 1/4 inch layer of clean coarse sand or fine gravel. Water sparingly afterward.

steps.title: Let topsoil dry between waterings
steps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

steps.title: Run a fan on the area indoors
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Pull mushrooms up by the stem and discard

homeRemedies.title: Hydrogen Peroxide Soil Drench
homeRemedies.recipe: 1 part 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide to 3 parts water. (Or 1 part 35% food-grade peroxide to 30 parts water.)
homeRemedies.usage: Pour around the base of the plant until the topsoil is moistened. Repeat once a week until the soil dries normally and bubbling stops.

homeRemedies.title: Cinnamon Powder
homeRemedies.recipe: Plain ground cinnamon from the spice rack. No mixing.
homeRemedies.usage: Dust a thin layer over moldy soil, on freshly cut stems, or around the base of seedlings. Cinnamon is naturally antifungal and rooting-friendly.

steps.title: Avoid keeping soil constantly damp
steps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

steps.title: Bottom-water if the plant tolerates it
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Improve airflow indoors
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Keep a layer of sand or gravel on top
steps.body: Scrape away the top 1/8 inch of moldy or salt-crusted soil and replace it with a 1/4 inch layer of clean coarse sand or fine gravel. Water sparingly afterward.

R04.name: Pot-bound roots
R04.shortDescription: Roots circling the pot, plant stalled or wilting
R04.symptomAnalysis:
When a plant outgrows its pot, roots circle the wall, then loop back over themselves. The root ball becomes denser than the soil and water can no longer be retained — it just runs down the side. Nutrient uptake drops, growth stalls, and the plant looks chronically thirsty even right after watering.

Quick check: roots growing out of drainage holes, or soil that pulls away from the pot wall, are clear signs.

steps.title: Unpot the plant
steps.body: Slide the plant out of its pot, gently shake off loose soil, and rinse the root ball under lukewarm running water until you can clearly see individual roots.

steps.title: Use clean fingers or a chopstick to tease apart circling roots
steps.body: Gently pull them outward, away from the ball.

steps.title: Trim away any dead or kinked roots
steps.body: Cut away black, mushy, or shriveled roots with sterilized pruners. Healthy roots are white or tan and pliable. Cut back to clean, firm tissue.

steps.title: Move to a pot 1–2 sizes larger
steps.body: Move a root-bound plant into a pot 1–2 sizes larger. Tease apart circling roots before settling the plant in fresh mix. Water deeply once.

steps.title: Fill in with fresh potting mix
steps.body: Move the plant to a clean pot with drainage holes, using fresh sterile potting mix. Bury roots up to the same depth as before. Water lightly to settle the soil.

steps.title: Water deeply once to settle the new soil
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: Up-pot every 1–2 years for fast growers; every 2–3 years for slow growers
Choose a pot 1–2 sizes larger than the current root ball — not bigger
steps.body: Oversized pots stay wet too long.

steps.title: Always use pots with drainage holes
steps.body: Move the plant to a clean pot with drainage holes, using fresh sterile potting mix. Bury roots up to the same depth as before. Water lightly to settle the soil.

R05.name: Salt buildup
R05.shortDescription: White crust on soil, brown leaf tips
R05.symptomAnalysis:
Fertilizer salts (and minerals from tap water) accumulate in the soil over time. As water evaporates, salts crystallize on the surface. High salt concentrations damage root tips, which then can't take up water, and the leaves show drought-like brown crispy edges even when the soil is moist.

Common in indoor pots, drought-tolerant plants over-fertilized, and any pot that has gone a year without a deep flush.

steps.title: Scrape off any visible salt crust on the soil and pot rim
steps.body: Scrape away the top 1/8 inch of moldy or salt-crusted soil and replace it with a 1/4 inch layer of clean coarse sand or fine gravel. Water sparingly afterward.

steps.title: Flush soil with 4× the pot's volume of clean water
steps.body: Take the plant to a sink or outdoors. Pour clean water slowly through the pot — about 4 times the pot's volume — until water runs freely from the drainage holes. Repeat once a month if salts return.

steps.title: Trim browned leaf tips for cosmetics
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material. If buildup is severe and the plant is in distress: *Repot With Fresh Soil and Drainage* (see 3.1).

subSteps.title: Repot With Fresh Soil and Drainage
subSteps.body: Move the plant to a clean pot with drainage holes, using fresh sterile potting mix. Bury roots up to the same depth as before. Water lightly to settle the soil.

steps.title: Flush soil every 1–3 months
steps.body: Take the plant to a sink or outdoors. Pour clean water slowly through the pot — about 4 times the pot's volume — until water runs freely from the drainage holes. Repeat once a month if salts return.

steps.title: Use rainwater or distilled water if your tap water is hard or chlorinated
Fertilize at half the labeled rate for indoor plants
steps.body: More is not better.

steps.title: Skip fertilizer in winter when growth slows

R06.name: Damping off
R06.shortDescription: Seedlings collapse at the soil line
R06.symptomAnalysis:
Damping off is caused by several soil fungi (Pythium, Rhizoctonia, Fusarium) that attack the thin stems of seedlings right where they meet the wet soil. Conditions that favor it: cool temperatures, overly damp seed-starting mix, poor airflow, and unsterilized trays or seeds.

Once seedlings collapse, they cannot recover. The fight is to protect the rest of the tray.

steps.title: Pull and discard collapsed seedlings immediately
Bottom-water remaining seedlings
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Let the surface dry slightly between waterings
steps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

steps.title: Run a small fan on a low setting nearby
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Move under brighter light if seedlings are leggy and pale
steps.body: Hang or stand a full-spectrum LED grow light 30–60 cm above the plant, on a 12–14 hour timer. Especially useful in winter or in dim rooms.

homeRemedies.title: Hydrogen Peroxide Soil Drench
homeRemedies.recipe: 1 part 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide to 3 parts water. (Or 1 part 35% food-grade peroxide to 30 parts water.)
homeRemedies.usage: Pour around the base of the plant until the topsoil is moistened. Repeat once a week until the soil dries normally and bubbling stops.

homeRemedies.title: Cinnamon Powder
homeRemedies.recipe: Plain ground cinnamon from the spice rack. No mixing.
homeRemedies.usage: Dust a thin layer over moldy soil, on freshly cut stems, or around the base of seedlings. Cinnamon is naturally antifungal and rooting-friendly.

homeRemedies.title: Chamomile Tea Drench
homeRemedies.recipe: Steep 2 chamomile tea bags in 1 quart of just-boiled water. Cool to room temperature.
homeRemedies.usage: Pour around the base of seedlings and over the soil surface. Mild antifungal action protects germinating seeds and young roots.

steps.title: Sterilize seed trays before reuse
steps.body: Soak empty pots for 10 minutes in a 1:9 bleach-to-water solution, then rinse thoroughly with clean water and air-dry before refilling with fresh soil.

steps.title: Use fresh sterile seed-starting mix
steps.body: Don't reuse old potting soil.

steps.title: Sow at the depth listed on the seed packet — burying too deep stresses germination
Provide gentle airflow from day one
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Remove humidity domes after sprouts emerge

R07.name: Fungus gnat larvae
R07.symptomAnalysis:
Adult fungus gnats are nuisance flies, but their larvae feed on tender young roots and root hairs in the top inch of constantly damp soil. They thrive in overwatered houseplants and seedling trays. Adults lay eggs on the soil surface; larvae hatch and feed for 1–2 weeks before pupating.

For the adult flies see [P16 — Fungus gnats](../02_pests/fungus-gnats.md). This entry covers the larvae killing roots.

steps.title: Let the top inch of soil dry completely between waterings
steps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

steps.title: Bottom-water only
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Top-dress with 1/4 inch of sand to block adult egg-laying
steps.body: Scrape away the top 1/8 inch of moldy or salt-crusted soil and replace it with a 1/4 inch layer of clean coarse sand or fine gravel. Water sparingly afterward.

steps.title: Bt-i (a different Bt strain — *Bacillus thuringiensis israelensis*, sold as "Mosquito Bits") sprinkled or steeped into water kills larvae specifically
steps.body: Apply per label.

homeRemedies.usage: Pour around the base of the plant until the topsoil is moistened. Repeat once a week until the soil dries normally and bubbling stops.

homeRemedies.title: Apple Cider Vinegar Trap
homeRemedies.recipe: Pour 1/4 cup apple cider vinegar into a small yellow cup or saucer. Add 1 drop of dish soap to break surface tension. Cover with plastic wrap and poke small holes.
homeRemedies.usage: Place near the affected plant. Adult fungus gnats and fruit flies are attracted, fall in, and drown. Refresh every 3–4 days.

steps.title: Quarantine new plants and check the soil
steps.body: Keep newly purchased plants separate from your collection for 2 weeks. Check the undersides of leaves, leaf joints, and the top of the soil for pests and disease before integrating them.

steps.title: Improve airflow
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

FL01.name: Flower rot
FL01.symptomAnalysis:
Two main causes:
1. **Heavy rain or overwatering** — water sits in the bloom and rots the petals from the inside.
2. **Bacterial infection** — enters through small wounds and spreads quickly in warm humid weather.

If left alone, the rot can travel down the stem and infect leaves and surrounding plants.

steps.title: Pinch off all rotting blooms
steps.body: Use clean fingers or sterilized snips to remove rotting, wilted, or infected blooms at their base, before infection spreads to surrounding healthy buds.

steps.title: Sterilize fingers or snips between cuts
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Cut back any soft, discolored stem below the bloom
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Switch to base watering only
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: For bacterial spread, apply copper bactericide per label
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days. If most blooms and stems are infected: *Discard the Severely Infected Plant* (see 5.1).

subSteps.title: Discard the Severely Infected Plant
subSteps.body: When more than half the plant is affected or root rot is widespread, dig the plant up, seal it in a heavy trash bag, and dispose. Sterilize the area before replanting.

steps.title: Never spray flowers from above
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Space plants for airflow
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Clear fallen petals and debris promptly
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Pick disease-resistant cultivars
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

steps.title: Rotate annual flower beds
steps.body: Do not plant the same species — or its botanical relatives — in the same spot two years in a row. A 3-year rotation breaks the lifecycle of soil-borne diseases and pests.

FL02.name: Flower withering
FL02.shortDescription: Blooms drying brown after frost or end of season
FL02.symptomAnalysis:
Two main causes:
1. **Cold or frost damage** — sudden temperature drops freeze flower cells, causing browning. Many flowers cannot tolerate temperatures below 50°F (10°C).
2. **Normal end of life** — annual and perennial flowers wither at the end of their season as the plant goes to seed.

If withering happens after a cold snap, the cause is environmental. If it happens at the end of the season with seed pods forming, it is natural.

steps.title: Mist lightly before sun hits the frosted plant
steps.body: Mist the foliage daily, group plants together, set the pot on a tray of pebbles in shallow water, or run a humidifier. Aim for 40–60% relative humidity.

steps.title: Once thawed, pinch off badly damaged blooms
steps.body: Use clean fingers or sterilized snips to remove rotting, wilted, or infected blooms at their base, before infection spreads to surrounding healthy buds.

steps.title: Cover at night until danger of frost passes
steps.body: Cover vulnerable plants with a frost cloth, burlap, sheet, or row cover before nightfall. Bring potted plants indoors. Remove the cover the next morning.

steps.title: Pinch Off Affected Flowers and Buds
steps.body: Use clean fingers or sterilized snips to remove rotting, wilted, or infected blooms at their base, before infection spreads to surrounding healthy buds.

steps.title: Do nothing — natural cycle
Cover with frost cloth before cold nights
steps.body: Cover vulnerable plants with a frost cloth, burlap, sheet, or row cover before nightfall. Bring potted plants indoors. Remove the cover the next morning.

steps.title: Bring potted flowering plants indoors overnight in early spring or late fall
Choose cold-hardy varieties suited to your zone
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

steps.title: Do not fertilize with nitrogen after midsummer; new tender growth is more frost-vulnerable

FL03.name: Wilting flowers
FL03.symptomAnalysis:
Wilting in flowers can come from many directions:

- **Water stress** — too much or too little. Soft, papery petals from drought; mushy stems from overwatering.
- **Heat / strong sun** — petals scorch and curl on the sun-facing side.
- **Vascular wilt diseases** — soil-borne fungi like *Verticillium* or *Fusarium* clog the plant's water-carrying tissue. Stems wilt even though soil is moist.
- **Root rot** — see [R01](../05_roots/root-rot.md). Damaged roots can't supply water to flowers.

Determine the cause first. Stems that wilt only on hot afternoons but recover overnight are heat-stressed; stems that wilt and stay wilted regardless of soil moisture suggest disease or root damage.

steps.title: Test the soil with a finger: if dry → [S17](../shared/treatment-steps.md#s17-water-deeply-but-less-often)
steps.body: If wet → *Reduce Watering Frequency* (see 1.1).

subSteps.title: Reduce Watering Frequency
subSteps.body: Let the top 1–2 inches of soil dry between waterings. Stick a finger in to check before each watering. Many root and stem rot problems trace back to chronically wet soil.

steps.title: Move to afternoon shade if heat-stressed
steps.body: Relocate the plant to a north-facing window, under a tree, or behind shade cloth. Sunburn and leaf scorch usually heal once the plant cools off.

steps.title: Prune wilted foliage to reduce demand
steps.body: Cut off affected foliage and twigs with sterilized tools, cutting back into healthy tissue. Bag and discard cuttings — never compost diseased material.

steps.title: Sterilize tools between cuts
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting. If the cause is vascular wilt disease: *Discard the Severely Infected Plant* (see 4.1). The fungus lingers in soil for years.

subSteps.title: Discard the Severely Infected Plant
subSteps.body: When more than half the plant is affected or root rot is widespread, dig the plant up, seal it in a heavy trash bag, and dispose. Sterilize the area before replanting.

steps.title: Water deeply and consistently
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: Pick wilt-resistant cultivars (look for "VFN" on tomato/strawberry seeds)
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

steps.title: Rotate Crops Between Seasons
steps.body: Do not plant the same species — or its botanical relatives — in the same spot two years in a row. A 3-year rotation breaks the lifecycle of soil-borne diseases and pests.

steps.title: Mulch to keep soil moisture even
steps.body: Spread a 2–3 inch layer of bark, straw, or leaf mulch around the plant, keeping a small ring clear from the stem. Mulch blocks splash-up of soil pathogens and stabilizes moisture.

FL04.name: Bud drop
FL04.shortDescription: Flower buds form then fall before opening
FL04.symptomAnalysis:
A stress response, not a disease. The plant is conserving energy by aborting buds it cannot support. Common triggers:

- **Sudden environmental change** — moved indoors/outdoors, draft, sudden temperature shift.
- **Inconsistent watering** — letting soil dry too far, then flooding it.
- **Low humidity** — common indoors; tropical bloomers (gardenias, hibiscus) are sensitive.
- **Insufficient light** — buds form but don't get the energy to open.
- **Over-fertilizing** — too much nitrogen pushes leaf growth at the cost of flowers.
- **Pest pressure** — thrips and aphids feeding inside developing buds. See [02_pests/](../02_pests/).

steps.title: Establish a consistent watering rhythm
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: Raise humidity for tropical bloomers
steps.body: Mist the foliage daily, group plants together, set the pot on a tray of pebbles in shallow water, or run a humidifier. Aim for 40–60% relative humidity.

steps.title: Stop moving the plant
steps.body: Let it settle in one location.

steps.title: Pause fertilizer for 2–4 weeks; resume with a low-nitrogen bloom formula (high P, like 5-10-5)
Check buds for pests
steps.body: If found, see the relevant pest entry.

steps.title: Acclimate plants slowly when moving them
Water on a schedule, not on impulse
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

steps.title: Maintain 40–60% humidity for indoor bloomers
steps.body: Mist the foliage daily, group plants together, set the pot on a tray of pebbles in shallow water, or run a humidifier. Aim for 40–60% relative humidity.

steps.title: Use a balanced or bloom-formula fertilizer; avoid heavy nitrogen during budding
Don't repot a plant that is forming buds — they will drop from the disturbance

FL05.name: Petal blight
FL05.symptomAnalysis:
The fungus *Botrytis cinerea* (gray mold) attacks fading or damaged petals first, then spreads into healthy ones. Thrives in cool, humid, still air — early spring, late fall, or any rainy spell. Roses, peonies, dahlias, hydrangeas, and azaleas are common hosts.

For the same fungus on leaves and stems see [L23 — Gray mold](../01_leaves/gray-mold.md).

steps.title: Remove every infected bloom, including any partly open buds nearby
steps.body: Use clean fingers or sterilized snips to remove rotting, wilted, or infected blooms at their base, before infection spreads to surrounding healthy buds.

steps.title: Sterilize tools between cuts
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Clear fallen petals and leaves under the plant
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Apply copper fungicide if infection is widespread
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

steps.title: Open up airflow with selective pruning
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Water at the base
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Space and prune for airflow
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Remove faded blooms before they decay
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Pick cultivars with single-petal or open-form flowers
steps.body: When buying plants, look for cultivars labeled resistant to common diseases (e.g. "Verticillium-resistant tomatoes", "rust-resistant roses"). Local nurseries and seed packets list resistance codes.

FL06.name: Failure to bloom
FL06.shortDescription: Healthy plant produces few or no flowers
FL06.symptomAnalysis:
Not a disease. The plant has the energy to grow leaves but is not triggered to flower. Common causes:

- **Insufficient light** — most flowering plants need 6+ hours of bright light per day.
- **Too much nitrogen** — pushes leafy growth at the expense of flowers.
- **Wrong temperature cycle** — many bloomers need a cool-night cue (e.g., orchids, Christmas cactus).
- **Plant is too young** — perennials, fruit trees, and many shrubs can take 1–3 years before first bloom.
- **Recent pruning at the wrong time** — pruning off flower buds (some plants set buds the previous year on old wood).
- **Root-bound** — see [R04](../05_roots/pot-bound.md). Sometimes a tight pot triggers blooming, but extreme root-bind shuts it down.
- **Dry or inconsistent watering** — drought-stressed plants drop bud sites.

steps.title: Move to a brighter location
steps.body: Relocate the plant gradually to a south- or west-facing window or a sunnier spot in the garden. Move in stages over a week to avoid sun shock.

steps.title: Switch to a bloom-formula fertilizer (lower N, higher P, e.g., 5-10-5)
Research your specific plant's bloom trigger
steps.body: Christmas cactus needs cool nights and short days; gardenias need warm days and humid air.

steps.title: Avoid pruning until you confirm whether the species blooms on new wood or old wood

homeRemedies.title: Banana Peel Potassium Tea
homeRemedies.recipe: Steep 2–3 chopped banana peels in 1 quart of water for 48 hours. Strain.
homeRemedies.usage: Pour around the base of stressed or yellowing plants every 2–3 weeks. Light potassium boost; not a complete fertilizer.

steps.title: Match the plant to your light conditions before buying
Use a balanced or bloom-formula fertilizer; skip pure-nitrogen feeds on bloomers
Be patient with new perennials and shrubs
Water consistently
steps.body: Water slowly and thoroughly until the soil is moistened all the way through, then let it dry partly before the next watering. Encourages deeper roots and stronger plants.

FL07.name: Spotted buds
FL07.shortDescription: Brown or dark spots and blotches appearing on flower buds before they open.
FL07.symptomAnalysis:
Spotted buds usually trace back to fungal or bacterial infection — botrytis, anthracnose, or shot-hole pathogens — that has spread from leaves or fallen petals onto developing buds. Wet weather, dense planting, and contaminated debris around the plant accelerate the spread. Spotted buds either fail to open, open into deformed blooms, or rot before showing color.

If the issue appears on petals after the bud opens (not the bud surface itself), see [FL05 — Petal blight](petal-blight.md).

steps.title: Pinch off any spotted buds and dispose
steps.body: Use clean fingers or sterilized snips to remove rotting, wilted, or infected blooms at their base, before infection spreads to surrounding healthy buds.

steps.title: Sterilize tools between cuts so spores don't move from bud to bud
steps.body: Dip pruners, scissors, or knife blades into a 1:4 bleach-to-water solution (or 70% rubbing alcohol) before every cut and between every plant. Wipe dry before cutting.

steps.title: Clear fallen petals and leaves around the plant
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Pinch off all visibly affected buds
steps.body: Use clean fingers or sterilized snips to remove rotting, wilted, or infected blooms at their base, before infection spreads to surrounding healthy buds.

steps.title: Spray copper fungicide on remaining buds and foliage
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.

steps.title: Space plants for airflow
steps.body: Space plants apart so leaves do not touch when fully grown. Indoors, run a small oscillating fan part of the day. Outdoors, prune neighboring vegetation that blocks breeze.

steps.title: Water at the base, never overhead
steps.body: Direct water onto the soil near the stem rather than spraying leaves. Use a long-spout watering can, soaker hose, or drip line. Wet leaves invite fungal and bacterial spread.

steps.title: Clear fallen petals and dead leaves regularly
steps.body: Rake fallen leaves, fruit, and dead branches out from under plants — especially in autumn. Many pathogens overwinter in plant debris and reinfect in spring.

steps.title: Preventive copper spray in early spring on susceptible roses or peonies
steps.body: Spray a copper-based fungicide (Bordeaux mixture, copper sulfate, or commercial copper hydroxide) on leaves, stems, and surrounding soil per the product label. Reapply every 7–14 days.
